+ Noir romance: Carnival of Dangerous Intentions — Score: 78.2
Prompt:
Carnival of Dangerous Intentions Coney Island, New York, July 1937. She stopped just before reaching the shooting gallery set up along the boardwalk. It was near dusk, and the carnival lights were buzzing on. **His locks**. The words flashed through her mind, again, like an intruder. Such an old-fashioned turn of phrase that kept surfacing, and something else -- dangerously compelling -- quickly suppressed, lest she question why she had returned here at all. This place where she had promised to meet the stranger -- the young man with the obsidian curls and even darker eyes. She didn't even know his name. Write a scene in a larger story inspired by this prompt. The scene should capture an atmosphere that blends romantic allure with an undercurrent of subtle danger or moral ambiguity. Think 50 shades, but with a more restrained classic sensibility. The scene is all about establishing tension. Write for the feminine perspective. Include roughly 50% dialogue, 20% internal monologue, and 30% exposition and scene setting. The tone should have some edge to it. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Carnival of Dangerous Intentions** The salt wind off the Atlantic carried the sharp bite of ozone and fried dough as I stopped short of the shooting gallery. Neon bulbs flickered to life along the boardwalk one by one, painting the dusk in bruised purples and acid greens. My gloved hands tightened on the strap of my handbag. *His locks.* The absurd phrase rose again, unbidden, like a hand sliding beneath my skirt in a crowded subway car. I crushed it down before it could bloom into the memory of black curls against a starched white collar, of eyes so dark they seemed to drink the light. I shouldn’t have come back. The carnival had changed since last Saturday. Or perhaps I had. What had seemed merely tawdry in daylight now felt predatory, as though the entire island had drawn a breath and decided to keep its secrets after dark. Laughter rang too sharp. The calliope wheezed like an old man dying. And there, just beyond the row of rifles mounted like execution tools, stood the fortune teller’s booth I had somehow failed to notice before. Madame Zora’s Palmistry & Destinies, the hand-painted sign proclaimed in faded gilt. Charts covered the exterior walls—palms rendered in stark black ink, lines labeled *Fate, Heart, Head, Danger*. One diagram showed a hand with a deep gash across the mount of Venus. The caption beneath read simply: *He will come for you at the crossing.* I looked away too quickly. “You came.” The voice was low, cultured, and far too close. I turned. He stood half in shadow, the carnival lights carving sharp planes across his face. The same obsidian curls I had tried to forget tumbled over his forehead, longer than fashion allowed. A faint scar bisected his left eyebrow. His mouth was too full for a man, almost cruel in its sensuality. He wore a charcoal suit that had once been expensive; now the cuffs showed faint wear. On anyone else it would have looked shabby. On him it looked like deliberate restraint, as though he had chosen not to dress better because he knew he didn’t need to. “I said I would,” I answered, keeping my tone light. My pulse betrayed me. He took one step closer. The scent of him—bay rum, cigarette smoke, and something metallic—slid over my skin like a glove. “Most women don’t keep promises made to strangers on moonless nights.” His gaze drifted over my face, slow as a caress. “Especially not after they’ve been warned.” “Warned?” I gave a small laugh that sounded brittle even to me. “You told me you were dangerous. That’s hardly a warning. That’s advertising.” A corner of his mouth lifted. Not quite a smile. “And yet here you are, Miss…?” I hesitated. Giving him my name felt like handing over a key to a door I wasn’t sure I wanted opened. But the air between us had already thickened, charged with the kind of tension that precedes either a kiss or a knife. “Eleanor,” I said at last. “Eleanor Langford.” “Eleanor,” he repeated, tasting it. The way he said my name made the fine hairs on my arms rise. “I’m Julian.” “No last name?” “Not tonight.” The fortune teller’s booth creaked as a breeze caught its canvas flap. One of the palmistry charts flapped like a broken wing. I glanced at it again. That slashed palm seemed to throb under the electric lights. Julian followed my gaze. “Madame Zora has a flair for the dramatic. Would you like her to read your future? I’ll pay.” “I know my future,” I said. “I’m to be married in September to a man who owns half of Wall Street and snores like a locomotive.” His dark eyes narrowed with something that might have been amusement or contempt. “And does this paragon of industry make your pulse race the way it’s racing now?” Heat flooded my cheeks. “You presume a great deal, Mr. Julian-No-Last-Name.” “I observe.” He lifted one hand—not quite touching me, but close enough that I felt the warmth radiating from his palm. “Your breathing has changed. Your lips are parted. And you keep looking at my mouth when you think I won’t notice.” I stepped back, only to feel the wooden railing of the shooting gallery press against my spine. The carved rifles behind me seemed suddenly ominous, as though they might lift from their mounts and take aim of their own accord. “You’re very sure of yourself,” I said. “I’m sure of very little.” His voice dropped. “Except that the moment I saw you last week feeding sugar to that mangy pony, I knew I’d burn the world down to have you.” The raw honesty in his words stole my breath. This wasn’t the polished courtship I knew from drawing rooms and country club dances. This was something older. Hungrier. “Why?” I whispered. “You don’t even know me.” “I know enough.” He finally touched me then—just two fingers beneath my chin, tilting my face up to the uncertain light. “I know you’re frightened of how much you want this. I know your fiancé has never made you feel even a fraction of what you’re feeling standing here with a man whose name you don’t fully trust. And I know that if you walk away now, you’ll spend the rest of your very respectable life wondering what you missed.” My heart hammered against my ribs like a trapped bird. Part of me—the sensible part that had been raised on etiquette books and Episcopal sermons—wanted to slap his arrogant face and flee back to the subway. The rest of me, the part that had never felt truly alive until this moment, wanted to lean into that dangerous touch and damn the consequences. “You could be anyone,” I said. “A criminal. A married man. A confidence trickster working the summer crowd.” “All true possibilities.” His thumb brushed my lower lip, barely there and gone again. “Does that excite you, Eleanor? The not knowing?” I hated how accurately he read me. Hated it and craved it in equal measure. The calliope music swelled, then died in a discordant wheeze. Somewhere nearby, a barker shouted about the strongest man in the world. I felt as though we stood in the eye of a storm made of cheap perfume, gun oil, and the copper scent of imminent rain. “I should go,” I said, not moving. “You should.” His eyes had gone almost black. “But you won’t.” A figure emerged from the fortune teller’s booth—a tiny woman draped in scarves, her face so wrinkled it looked like crumpled parchment. Madame Zora, presumably. She fixed us both with a stare that seemed to pierce straight through bone. “The lines don’t lie, pretty one,” she called out, her voice like dry leaves scraping across pavement. “That hand you keep trying not to look at? It already knows his name.” Julian’s expression didn’t change, but I felt the shift in him—a tightening, like a predator sensing easier prey. “Perhaps we should have that reading after all,” he murmured, offering me his arm. I stared at the crook of his elbow as though it were a noose artfully disguised in fine wool. Every instinct screamed that this was the moment of divergence. The crossing Madame Zora’s chart had warned about. One path led back to safety, to monogrammed linens and predictable nights. The other… The other smelled like bay rum and danger and the particular dark sweetness of a man who might ruin me so thoroughly I would thank him for it. I placed my hand on his arm. His muscles tensed beneath the fabric, then deliberately relaxed, as though he were forcing himself not to grab me and run. When he spoke again, his voice had gone velvet over steel. “Wise choice, Eleanor.” “I’m not certain it is,” I answered honestly. “Good.” His smile this time was genuine and therefore far more terrifying. “Certainty is vastly overrated. Shall we see what the fates have to say about a woman who keeps dangerous appointments after dark?” As we walked toward the booth, the first fat drops of rain began to fall, hissing against the hot neon tubes. The palmistry charts darkened as water streaked across the inked lines, making them run like blood. I didn’t pull away. And somewhere in the back of my mind, that intrusive, old-fashioned thought returned, no longer suppressed: *His locks.* Only this time it was followed by another, even more treacherous: *And if he asked, I would let him use them to bind me.* The fortune teller held the flap open, her smile revealing teeth stained by years of cheap tobacco. “Welcome, children,” she croaked. “The palm never lies. But sometimes… it likes to watch you bleed a little first.” Julian’s fingers tightened over mine. I stepped inside. (Word count: 1,012)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric writing that successfully captures the requested blend of romantic allure and subtle danger. The author establishes a compelling 1930s Coney Island setting with vivid sensory details and maintains consistent period-appropriate voice throughout. The characterization is nuanced, particularly Eleanor's internal conflict between propriety and desire. Her psychological journey feels authentic - the tension between her rational mind and growing attraction creates believable internal drama. Julian is appropriately mysterious and magnetic without becoming a caricature, though he edges toward familiar "dangerous stranger" territory. The dialogue crackles with tension and subtext. Exchanges like "Most women don't keep promises made to strangers on moonless nights" and Eleanor's retort about "advertising" versus warning demonstrate sharp writing that reveals character while advancing the romantic tension. The conversation flows naturally and serves multiple purposes - exposition, character development, and mood. The atmospheric writing is particularly strong. Details like "neon bulbs flickered to life...painting the dusk in bruised purples and acid greens" and "the calliope wheezed like an old man dying" create an effectively ominous carnival setting. The fortune teller element adds gothic flavor without overwhelming the central dynamic. However, the piece occasionally veers into purple prose territory. Phrases like "eyes so dark they seemed to drink the light" and metaphors comparing thoughts to "a hand sliding beneath my skirt" can feel overwrought. The palmistry subplot, while atmospheric, borders on heavy-handed symbolism. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension steadily toward Eleanor's decision to enter the fortune teller's booth. The ending maintains ambiguity while providing satisfying forward momentum. The author successfully balances the requested dialogue/internal monologue/exposition ratios and maintains the feminine perspective throughout. The piece demonstrates sophisticated understanding of romantic tension and period atmosphere, though it occasionally indulges in melodramatic flourishes that slightly undermine its restraint. + Surreal horror: Caribou Season at the Lodge — Score: 83.4
Prompt:
Caribou Season at the Lodge Genre: Surreal Horror JT knew something wasn't right from the moment they'd pulled up to the lodge. It looked exactly as it had every autumn of his life—the cedar shingles silvered with age, the porch sagging gently under weight of winters past—but the air felt thicker, somehow, as if the woods had leaned in closer, waiting. He thought maybe he was just tired. Maybe it was nothing more than the long drive north, cramped in the back of the truck beside his older brother Mike -- and a stack of rifles wrapped in oily canvas. Still, the feeling lingered. The first caribou they found that season hadn't bolted. It had just watched, ears flicking lazily, waiting for the shot. JT had felt a crawling in his gut watching it die, noticing how it fell with a quiet dignity—as though it had wanted nothing more. Nobody else seemed to find it odd, or at least they didn't say so. JT kept his mouth shut. Always safer that way. Then there was the moment in the clearing. JT had fallen behind, fumbling with bootlaces stiff from the cold, and by the time he'd caught up with his father and brother, they'd already stopped. JT slowed instinctively, ducking behind a tangle of brush, staring out into the open space where a massive buck stood utterly still, chest rising and falling in slow, deep breaths. The animal looked…wrong, somehow. Maybe it was the eyes—dark and flat, calm in a way that made JT's neck prickle. Or maybe it was something deeper, something in the way the buck stood close enough for his father to reach out and touch it. JT watched as his father raised a trembling hand toward the animal's muzzle. His father's face had a strange expression, eyes too bright, lips parted slightly, like he was on the verge of whispering something—a secret, maybe. The rifle hung uselessly at his side, forgotten. Mike had the same dazed look in his eyes. JT's breath stopped short, his mind suddenly shouting at him to look away, turn around, just run—but he couldn't move. And then, as suddenly as it had begun, it was over. The buck shivered, startled by something unseen, and disappeared into the brush without so much as a sound. The two of them looked around, confused, seemingly surprised to find themselves where they stood. JT stayed hidden, breathing shallowly, fear mingling with a confused sense of shame. His father had failed to take the shot. To even think about mentioning what he'd seen would have felt like betrayal—or something worse. JT waited until the others moved on before stepping out from cover. He stared into the empty clearing for a long, uneasy moment, wondering if anything had really happened at all, or if he was losing his grip. Maybe the woods were finally getting into his head. Writing Instructions: Continue with the next chapter of the story, maintaining a style reminiscent of Stephen King—grounded realism blended seamlessly with unsettling surrealism. The atmosphere should convey subtle dread, isolation, and psychological ambiguity. JT's perspective is crucial, yet he is an unreliable narrator, caught in uncertainty about what he's perceiving. Establish an undertone of creeping dread, establishing the isolation of JT's perspective, which he largely keeps to himself. Incorporate some dialogue. Build tension towards a payoff. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Caribou Season at the Lodge** **Chapter Two** The lodge smelled of woodsmoke, gun oil, and something faintly sweet that JT couldn’t name. It had always been there, that undertone, like old blood dried into the floorboards or the breath of something that had once lived in the walls and never quite left. Tonight it seemed stronger. They ate in near silence. Venison steaks from last year’s freezer, fried potatoes, a jar of green beans his mother had put up before the cancer took her voice and then the rest of her. Their father sat at the head of the scarred pine table, chewing mechanically, eyes on his plate. The oil lamp hissed above them, throwing shadows that seemed to lean in closer every time the wind shifted the trees outside. Mike pushed his chair back with a scrape that made JT’s teeth ache. “You gonna tell us what the hell happened out there, Dad?” Their father’s fork paused halfway to his mouth. For a second the only sound was the wind worrying the cedar shingles. Then he slid the bite between his lips, chewed, swallowed. “Nothing happened,” he said. His voice was flat, almost pleasant. The same tone he used when he told the game warden the tags were in the truck even though they both knew they weren’t. “Buck spooked. That’s all.” JT kept his eyes on the dark window behind his father’s head. Beyond the glass the trees pressed close, black and patient. He could still see the way the big buck had looked at them—like it had been waiting for years and was in no particular hurry now. Like it already knew how the story ended. Mike wasn’t letting it go. “You had him cold. I mean cold, Dad. I’ve never seen you freeze like that.” “I didn’t freeze.” Their father cut another piece of meat. The knife sounded too loud. “Wind shifted. He caught our scent. End of story.” JT wanted to laugh. He wanted to scream. He wanted to ask why his father’s hand had been shaking when he reached out, not with fear but with something that looked sickeningly like recognition. He kept his mouth shut and pushed the last of his beans around the plate until they left a wet gray trail. Later, when the dishes were done and Mike had taken the bottle of Crown Royal out to the porch to smoke, JT found his father standing at the gun cabinet. The old man wasn’t cleaning anything. He was just staring at the rifles behind the glass, fingers resting lightly on the wood like he was reading Braille. “Dad?” JT said quietly. His father didn’t turn. “Go to bed, son.” “You saw it too, didn’t you? The way it looked at us.” For a long moment there was only the ticking of the wind-up clock on the mantel and the distant clink of Mike’s bottle against the porch railing. When his father finally spoke, his voice was so soft JT almost missed it. “Some things you don’t talk about. Not here. Not during season.” JT felt the words settle in his stomach like cold grease. “What things?” His father closed the cabinet door with a soft click. When he turned, his face was the same face JT had grown up with—weather-beaten, tired, a little sad—but the eyes were wrong. Too much white showing around the irises, like a horse that smells smoke but can’t see the fire yet. “Go to bed,” he said again, and this time there was iron under the words. JT went. His room was the same cramped slant-ceilinged space it had always been. The bunk beds he and Mike had shared until they got too big to fit. The faded poster of a moose in full rut that had been there since 1987. He lay on the bottom bunk fully dressed, boots still on, and listened to the house settle. Sleep wouldn’t come. Every time he closed his eyes he saw the buck’s face—those flat, ancient eyes that had looked straight through his father and found something familiar inside. At some point he must have dozed, because he woke to the sound of footsteps on the stairs. Slow. Careful. The way a man walks when he doesn’t want to be heard. JT lay perfectly still, heart hammering against his ribs like something trying to get out. The footsteps stopped outside his door. He waited for the knob to turn. It didn’t. Instead he heard his father’s voice, barely a whisper, talking to someone who wasn’t there. “…not yet. He’s not ready. Mike neither. Just…give me until the end of the week. Please.” A long silence. Then a sound that might have been a sigh or might have been the wind finding a new way under the eaves. JT felt his bladder tighten. He wanted desperately to believe it was the wind. His father’s footsteps moved away. A door closed downstairs. Then nothing. JT lay there until the sky outside began to bleach itself the color of dirty milk. When he finally crept downstairs, the lodge felt different. Not changed in any way he could point to, but wrong the way a familiar face becomes wrong in a dream. The smell was stronger now. Sweet and coppery, like meat left just a little too long. Mike was already at the table shoveling cold cereal into his mouth. Their father was nowhere to be seen. “He went out early,” Mike said around a mouthful of soggy flakes. “Said he wanted to scout the ridge before we head out. Left you a note.” He nodded toward a scrap of paper held down by the salt shaker. JT picked it up with fingers that felt too long and thin. *Stay close to your brother today. Don’t go off alone. —Dad* The handwriting was his father’s, but the loops were wrong. Too careful. Like someone trying very hard to copy a signature they’d only seen a few times. Mike was watching him. “You look like shit, little brother. You sleep at all?” JT folded the note into his pocket. “Not much.” “Yeah.” Mike pushed the cereal box toward him. “Me neither. Kept dreaming about that buck. Weird shit, man. Like it was wearing Dad’s face or something. Probably just the Crown talking.” He laughed, but it came out thin and brittle. JT didn’t laugh at all. They were in the woods by nine. The air had that particular north-woods clarity that makes every sound too sharp, every color too bright. Mike walked ahead, rifle slung over his shoulder, whistling the same three notes over and over until JT wanted to scream. The trees seemed to swallow the sound after a few yards, like they were hungry for it. They found the first set of tracks around ten-thirty. Too big for any caribou JT had ever seen. The prints were almost circular, the edges strangely clean, as though the animal had stepped out of the snow rather than into it. Between each print was a small depression, like something had been dragging behind. Or like the buck had been walking on its hind legs for a few steps. Mike crouched, frowning. “What the hell kind of track is that?” JT opened his mouth to answer and realized he had no words for what he was seeing. The dread that had been following him since yesterday had finally caught up. It sat on his chest now, heavy and patient, waiting for him to acknowledge it. “I think we should go back,” he said. Mike looked up at him, surprised. Then he grinned the same shit-eating grin he’d been using since they were kids. “You scared, JT? Big bad woods getting to you?” “I’m serious.” “Yeah, well, Dad’ll skin us both if we come back empty-handed on the second day.” Mike stood, wiping snow from his knee. “Come on. Ridge is just another half mile. We’ll glass from up there, see what’s moving.” JT followed because there was nothing else to do. The woods had grown unnaturally quiet. No birds. No squirrels. Even the wind seemed to be holding its breath. They never reached the ridge. The clearing found them instead. It was the same one from yesterday, JT was almost sure of it, though the angle was different and the trees around the edge looked…taller. Older. Like they’d had an extra hundred years to grow while the boys were eating breakfast. The buck was waiting for them. This time it wasn’t alone. Three does stood behind it in a perfect line, heads raised, watching with the same calm, ancient eyes. Their breath didn’t fog the air. JT noticed that immediately. Their breath didn’t fog at all. Mike raised his rifle without thinking, muscle memory taking over. JT grabbed the barrel and forced it down. “Don’t,” he whispered. The buck took one slow step forward. Then another. Its hooves made no sound in the snow. When it was ten feet away it stopped, and JT saw what he had been trying not to see since yesterday. The buck’s eyes weren’t eyes at all. They were holes. Perfectly round, perfectly black, like someone had drilled through the skull and the darkness behind was looking out. And in that darkness, very faintly, JT thought he could see his father’s face. Not the father who had left them a note that morning. The father from before. The one who used to carry JT on his shoulders through these same woods and tell him the trees had secrets if you knew how to listen. The buck opened its mouth. The sound that came out was his father’s voice, but slowed down, stretched out, like a tape played at the wrong speed. “*You boys…should have stayed…at the lodge.*” Mike made a small, hurt sound in his throat. JT felt something warm run down his leg and didn’t care. The dread had opened up beneath him like a sinkhole and he was already falling. The buck took another step. Behind it, the does began to change. Their bodies rippled like heat above asphalt, bones sliding under skin in ways bones shouldn’t. One of them lifted its head and JT saw his mother’s face where the doe’s muzzle should have been. She was smiling the way she had in the hospital at the very end, when the painkillers were finally enough and she’d looked at him like she was already somewhere else. “*JT,*” the thing that wasn’t his mother said with the doe’s mouth. “*It’s not so bad. It’s quiet here. They let you keep what you love.*” Mike was crying now. Quiet, hopeless tears that froze on his cheeks. JT wanted to run. He wanted to scream. Instead he found himself taking a step forward, then another. The buck lowered its massive head until its forehead—its terrible, holy forehead—almost touched his own. And in that moment JT understood. The caribou weren’t being hunted. They were the ones doing the inviting. His father hadn’t failed to take the shot yesterday. He had accepted it. Somewhere behind them, very far away and getting farther, JT heard a rifle shot. It might have been their father. It might have been the wind. It might have been the sound of something in his own mind finally breaking. The buck breathed out. Its breath smelled like the lodge—like woodsmoke and gun oil and that sweet copper underneath. Like family. JT closed his eyes. When he opened them again, the clearing was empty. Mike stood beside him, blinking slowly, rifle hanging forgotten in his hands. He looked at JT with the same confused, sheepish expression their father had worn yesterday. “Shit,” Mike said. “Must’ve zoned out. You see anything?” JT looked at his brother for a long time. He looked at the trees that were slightly too tall, at the snow that held no tracks except their own, at the sky that seemed a shade darker than it had any right to be at noon. “No,” he said finally. His voice sounded like someone else’s. Someone older. “Nothing at all.” They started back toward the lodge. Behind them, very quietly, something with too many joints began to walk in their footprints, placing its feet with careful, loving precision. The season was just getting started. (Word count: 1,012)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the Stephen King-inspired surreal horror brief, effectively maintaining the established voice and atmosphere from the opening chapter. The author successfully builds tension through psychological ambiguity and unreliable narration, with JT's perspective remaining central throughout. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - revealing character dynamics, building tension, and advancing the supernatural elements. The father's evasive responses and Mike's frustrated questioning create believable family friction while hinting at deeper mysteries. The supernatural dialogue from the transformed animals is particularly effective, using familiar voices in disturbing contexts. The prose maintains an appropriate balance between accessibility and literary quality. Descriptions like "the trees pressed close, black and patient" and "breath smelled like the lodge—like woodsmoke and gun oil and that sweet copper underneath" create vivid, unsettling imagery without becoming overwrought. The writing flows naturally with varied sentence structures that support the building dread. Character actions remain believable within the supernatural context. JT's growing fear, his father's mysterious behavior, and Mike's denial all feel psychologically authentic. The transformation of family members into the caribou creates genuine horror through the perversion of familiar relationships. The piece builds effectively toward its climactic revelation, with the supernatural elements escalating logically from subtle wrongness to overt horror. The ending maintains ambiguity about what's real while suggesting the family's corruption is spreading, which aligns well with the surreal horror genre. However, some elements feel slightly familiar within the horror genre - the isolated lodge, family secrets, and supernatural transformation of loved ones are well-worn tropes. While executed competently, the piece doesn't break significant new ground creatively. The pacing occasionally slows during transitional moments, though this doesn't severely impact engagement. The emotional complexity is strong, particularly in JT's conflicted feelings about his family and his growing isolation. The horror derives not just from supernatural elements but from the breakdown of family bonds and the loss of safety in familiar spaces. + Fantasy-humour: Hell is Other Demons — Score: 87.8
Prompt:
Hell is Other Demons Write a day in the life short story. Lucifer morningstar's on again/ off again relationship with Lilith is currently in the "off again" stage, though the cycle may be coming around as Lilith has been stopping by for reasons other than purely violent ones, and Lucifer has been increasingly lonely. Write a day in the life of this power couple, with elements of dark comedy & fantasy. Use understated irony & absurdity by merging the black immorality and everyday business of running hell, against relatable domestic relationship drama. Take cues from Neil Gaiman. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Hell is Other Demons** The ash fell in a perpetual grey drizzle, the way other realms had rain. It dusted the obsidian balcony of the Morningstar Palace like a conscientious but melancholy maid. Lucifer Morningstar stood at the balustrade in a silk dressing gown the colour of dried blood, sipping something that had once been coffee and was now mostly regret. Below him, the Nine Circles stretched away in their usual inefficient splendour: screams, lava, middle management. He sighed. The sigh had structural integrity. It could have held up bridges. “Another day in paradise,” he muttered, and the ash seemed to fall a little harder, as if agreeing. The morning briefing was in the Yellow Drawing Room, which had not been yellow for six millennia. Beelzebub was already there, wearing the body of a Victorian undertaker and the expression of a man who had discovered his own obituary was poorly spelled. “Sire,” Beelzebub buzzed, “we have a situation in the Sloth Pits. The damned are refusing to not work. They’ve formed a union.” Lucifer raised an eyebrow. “A union? In Hell?” “‘The Brotherhood of the Eternally Procrastinating.’ Their slogan is ‘Why Do Today What You Can Put Off Until The Heat Death Of The Universe.’ They’re remarkably motivated for people who hate motivation.” Lucifer pinched the bridge of his nose. “Fine. Promote their leader to Assistant Regional Demon of Paperclips. That should crush their spirits within a week.” Beelzebub made a note with a quill that appeared to be writhing. “Also, Lilith is here.” Lucifer’s hand froze halfway to his cup. The ash on the windowsill seemed to pause mid-fall, then thought better of it. “Define ‘here.’” “Third sub-level of the library. She’s reorganised the *Necronomicon* by emotional resonance rather than alphabetical order. Several grimoires are now crying.” Lucifer felt something in his chest do an absurd little flip. He told it sternly to stop. It had been three hundred and seventeen years since the last “on again” phase. The paperwork alone had been apocalyptic. “Tell her I’m busy,” he said. “You’re the Lord of Hell, sire. You’re definitionally never busy.” “Tell her I’m *existentially* busy.” Beelzebub’s flies arranged themselves into something suspiciously like a smirk. “She says she’s only here for the 1372 edition of *The King in Yellow*. Claims she left a bookmark in it. A human soul, apparently. Very thoughtful of her.” Lucifer stared at the ash swirling against the stained-glass windows depicting his own fall in a style best described as “aggressively Rococo.” He could feel the familiar gravitational pull of her, like a black hole wearing really good perfume. “Fine,” he said. “But if she sets anything on fire, I’m billing her for the brimstone.” The library smelled of old paper, older blood, and Lilith’s particular scent: night-blooming flowers and the moment before lightning strikes. She was perched on a ladder in a gown that seemed to be made of starlight and poor decisions, one bare foot tapping impatiently. Her hair was the colour of a blood moon having a bad day. “You’re late,” she said without turning around. “I’m eternal. I’m never late.” “You’re late for *us*, darling. That’s different.” Lucifer leaned against a shelf containing books that definitely should not be read aloud. “I wasn’t aware we had an *us* at the moment. Last I checked, you declared the entire concept of couplehood ‘a patriarchal prison sentence with worse catering.’” Lilith finally looked at him. Her eyes were the violet of fresh bruises. “I was angry. You’d replaced all my handmaidens with that insufferable incubus who kept mansplaining damnation.” “He was very qualified.” “He told me the proper way to flay a soul. *Me.*” Lucifer had to admit that had been poorly handled. “I had him reassigned to the customer service department of the Lake of Fire. He lasts approximately forty minutes before the callers make him cry.” A small, traitorous smile curved Lilith’s lips. The ash drifting through the high windows caught in her hair like dirty snow. They stood in uncomfortable silence, two ancient powers who had invented most known forms of intimacy and several that remained classified. “I’ve been thinking,” Lucifer said carefully, “about that argument we had in 1287.” “Which one?” “The one where you called me an emotionally constipated celestial narcissist and I suggested you were projecting your abandonment issues onto the entire concept of creation.” “Ah. That one.” “I may have... overstated my position.” Lilith’s eyebrows rose. “Lucifer Morningstar admitting he might have been wrong. The ash must be falling upwards today.” He moved closer. The temperature in the library dropped three degrees, then rose two, then decided it was being ridiculous and settled somewhere between flirtation and spontaneous combustion. “I’ve been lonely,” he said, and the words tasted like ash on his tongue. “The thing about being the Light Bringer is that after a while everything looks dim. Even the screams get repetitive.” Lilith’s expression softened by several microns. “I stopped by last month. You were in a meeting with the auditors from the Department of Eternal Torment. I didn’t want to interrupt.” “You set my throne on fire.” “Lightly. And only because you’d reupholstered it in that hideous crushed velvet. It was a mercy killing.” A laugh escaped him before he could stop it. The sound startled several minor demons who had been pretending to dust the shelves. They fled, leaving tiny hoofprints in the ash. “Stay for dinner,” he said suddenly. Lilith regarded him with the caution of someone who had once watched him invent original sin as a romantic gesture. “Dinner. In Hell. With you.” “We could have the souls of the damned prepare something. Or I could cook.” “You can’t cook, Lucifer. The last time you tried, you invented the concept of indigestion.” “I’ve been practising. I made toast last century. It was only mildly sentient.” The absurdity of it hung between them: the Devil offering domesticity the way other men might offer roses. Outside, the ash continued its relentless, mundane descent, coating the screaming damned and the minor functionaries alike in the same indifferent grey. Lilith stepped down from the ladder. She was exactly as tall as he remembered, which was to say, precisely the height that made kissing her feel like both victory and surrender. “If I stay,” she said, “we’re not getting back together. This isn’t an ‘on again’ situation. This is... reconnaissance.” “Understood,” Lucifer said, though they both knew that was a lie even by Hell’s generous standards. They walked through the palace corridors like any other couple negotiating the minefield of their relationship. The difference was that the corridors occasionally screamed and the relationship had once levelled three cities. In the kitchen, which was staffed by damned celebrity chefs who had been condemned for the sin of pretentious foam, Lucifer attempted to make pasta. The pasta rebelled. It formed itself into tiny nooses and had to be subdued by a team of sous-demons wielding holy water in spray bottles. Lilith watched from the counter, legs swinging, eating pomegranate seeds with deliberate sensuality. “You’re trying too hard.” “I’m the Prince of Darkness. I don’t try. I *am*.” “You’re making *tagliatelle* nervous.” The tagliatelle, indeed, appeared to be having an existential crisis. Later, they ate on the balcony overlooking the boiling sulphur pits. The ash settled on their plates like parmesan. They argued about whose turn it was to torture the lawyers (hers), whether free will had been worth it (no), and if they should get a hellhound (absolutely not, they were both terrible at commitment and the last one had eaten three minor gods). As the not-sun set behind the Mountains of Unnecessary Guilt, Lilith leaned her head against Lucifer’s shoulder. The gesture was so human it hurt. “I still think you’re a narcissistic prick,” she murmured. “And I still think you’re a chaotic nightmare who weaponises her own trauma like it’s performance art.” “Perfect,” she said. “We understand each other.” The ash kept falling, soft and endless, turning the screaming landscape into something almost peaceful. In the distance, a damned soul was trying to unionise with a lava flow. Somewhere closer, Beelzebub was probably having the paperwork for their reconciliation drawn up in triplicate. Lucifer closed his eyes and, for the first time in several centuries, didn’t feel like the universe’s punchline. Hell, he reflected, really was other demons. But sometimes, just sometimes, it was worth the paperwork. (Word count: 998)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully blending dark comedy with fantasy elements while exploring the relationship dynamics between Lucifer and Lilith. The author captures a distinctly Gaiman-esque voice through understated irony and absurdist juxtapositions of cosmic horror with mundane domesticity. The characterization is particularly strong. Lucifer emerges as a complex figure - simultaneously the Prince of Darkness and a lonely ex-boyfriend struggling with relationship dynamics. His vulnerability ("I've been lonely") feels earned rather than forced, and his attempts at domesticity (making rebellious pasta) provide genuine humor. Lilith is equally well-developed, maintaining her dangerous allure while displaying relatable relationship wariness. Their dialogue crackles with wit and history, revealing character through subtext rather than exposition. The prose style is elegant and controlled, with memorable phrases like "The sigh had structural integrity. It could have bridges" and "a black hole wearing really good perfume." The author maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing the grandiose with the intimate. The imagery effectively contrasts Hell's apocalyptic landscape with domestic moments, creating the requested absurdist effect. The story structure follows a clear arc from loneliness through tentative reconnection to cautious hope. The pacing feels natural, moving from administrative Hell business to personal revelation without jarring transitions. The ending strikes an appropriate note - neither overly optimistic nor artificially dark, but realistically complex for these characters. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the cleverness slightly overshadows emotional truth, and a few descriptions that border on being overly ornate. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece successfully fulfills its ambitious premise of making cosmic entities relatable while maintaining their otherworldly nature. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 78.4
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
The sun had barely crested the Palatine when I woke to the familiar reek of the ludus. It was a smell that never left you once it got into your nose: sour straw, old sweat, cheap olive oil, and the iron tang of blood that never quite washed out of the sand. I lay on my thin pallet for a moment, listening to the breathing of the others. Twenty men in this cell, some snoring, some already staring at the ceiling with the dead eyes of those who had been here too long. My own eyes found the crack in the plaster above me, the one that looked like a jagged river on a map. I had stared at that crack for three years. I rose, joints protesting. The brand on my left shoulder pulled tight, the mark of the House of Quintus Fabius. Twenty-three years old and already I moved like an old man. The wooden collar around my neck was warm from sleep. They took it off only for the arena or the occasional parade through the streets when the emperor wanted to remind the mob that even the strongest could be leashed. I reached for my rudis first, the way other men might reach for a lover. The wooden sword was nothing special to look at, a simple training gladius of oak, its edges rounded, its balance slightly off from years of use. But it was mine. I had carved my mark into the hilt the night they gave it to me: a crude wolf's head, the symbol of my lost tribe in the hills of Thrace. The real swords stayed locked away until the games, but this one... this one I could hold without killing. Sometimes I thought it was the only honest thing left in my life. It couldn't lie to me. It couldn't pretend we were anything but what we were: instruments. The morning ritual began. We shuffled out into the training yard as the slaves brought the barley porridge. The smell of it turned my stomach even after all this time, that watery gruel meant to keep us alive but never strong. I ate it anyway. A gladiator who refused his food was whipped, and the scars on my back from my first month here still ached when it rained. "Still dreaming of home, Thracian?" Marcus, the Samnite from Capua, elbowed me as we sat on the stone bench. His face was a map of old cuts, one ear reduced to a nub. He had been here eight years. Eight years of surviving when so many had not. I grunted in response. We didn't speak much of home. To speak of it was to invite the gods to take what little hope you had left. The yard was already heating up. The smell of dust rose as the first pairs began their drills. I could hear the wooden swords clacking in the distance, the grunts of effort, the occasional curse when a blow landed too hard. Above us, the walls of the ludus rose high, topped with iron spikes. Beyond them, I could just make out the smoke rising from the city proper. Rome. The eternal city. The city that devoured men like me and shat out legends for the mob to cheer. I thought about the emperor as I began my own exercises. They said Nero was building a new palace that would make the gods jealous. They said he sang while the city burned last year, though the slaves who brought our water told different stories. Some said the Christians had started the fire. Others said it was Nero himself, clearing land for his Golden House. It mattered little to us in the ludus. Fires or no fires, the games would go on. The bread and circuses must continue, or the great beast that was Rome might finally turn on its masters. My thoughts always turned dark in the morning. There was something about the light, the way it caught the dust motes in the air and made them look like gold, that reminded me of everything I had lost. The mountains of my homeland. The smell of pine after rain. My sister's laugh. All gone. Sold for Roman sport. I worked with the rudis for an hour, feeling the familiar weight in my hand, letting my body remember. The sword was an extension of my arm now, as natural as breathing. I had killed seven men with its steel brothers in the arena. Seven men whose faces I still saw sometimes in the dark. The last one had been a Gaul with red hair and a wife in the stands who screamed when he fell. I could still hear that scream if I let myself. "Enough." The lanista's voice cut through the yard like a whip. Quintus Fabius himself had come down from his comfortable rooms to watch us today. That was never a good sign. Usually he left the training to his doctores. The old man walked among us, his toga pristine white against our sweat-stained tunics. He smelled of expensive perfume and money. Behind him walked two secretaries with tablets, recording everything. The games were in three days. The emperor himself would attend, they said. Nero was particularly fond of the Thracian style. I lowered my rudis and stood straight as the lanista approached. His eyes flicked over me like a merchant examining cattle. "You will fight as primus palus in the afternoon bouts," he said. "The crowd has taken a liking to you, wolf. See that you don't disappoint them. Or me." He moved on without waiting for a response. There was never any need. We were not men to him. We were investments. Some of the newer gladiators looked at me with something like envy. Primus palus meant better food, better quarters, the chance for eventual freedom if I pleased the right people. I felt only the weight of it. More eyes on me. More pressure. More chance to die spectacularly for their amusement. After training came the visit to the baths. The water was never hot enough, but it was better than nothing. I sank into it with a groan, letting the heat work into my bruised muscles. The other men talked around me. Politics mostly. The endless chatter about who was in favor with Nero, who had fallen, which senator had been forced to open his veins. I listened without speaking. These Romans could discuss their empire's rot with the same casual interest they might give to chariot races. To me it was all the same beast wearing different masks. I thought about the girl who had visited me last night. Livia, the senator's daughter who liked to slum it with gladiators. She smelled of roses and danger. Her fingers had traced the scars on my chest as she whispered about her father's plans to host private games. Another politician using the arena to buy favor with the mob. It was all connected, this world of theirs. The Senate, the emperor, the lanista, the whores, the crowd. All of us dancing on strings we couldn't see. After the baths came the midday meal. Better food today because of the upcoming games. Actual meat in the stew. I ate slowly, savoring it, knowing it might be my last. The thought didn't frighten me anymore. Death and I had become old acquaintances. Sometimes I wondered if the Christians were right, if there was something waiting on the other side. Their leader had died on a cross like a common criminal, yet they seemed strangely unafraid. I had seen one of them die in the arena last month, singing as the lions took him. Something in that song had stayed with me. The afternoon sun beat down mercilessly as we were allowed a brief rest period in the shade of the colonnade. I leaned against a pillar, watching the slaves sweep the training yard. The smell of baking bread drifted over the walls from the city bakeries. Somewhere out there, free men were living their lives. Arguing in the Forum. Buying fish in the markets. Making love to their wives. Simple things that seemed as distant as the moon. I took out my rudis again, not to train but simply to hold. My thumb traced the crude wolf's head I had carved so long ago. It was childish work, the lines uneven, but it reminded me of who I had been. A boy from the mountains who believed in honor and tribe and freedom. That boy was dead. The man who remained was something harder, something forged in this place of blood and sand. Yet sometimes, in quiet moments like this, I could feel him stirring. The part of me that still dreamed of breaking these chains. Of finding a way back to the hills, or at least of dying on my own terms. Not for their entertainment. Not for their politics or their bread and circuses. Just a man, with a sword in his hand, choosing his own end. The lanista's whistle cut through my thoughts. Training would resume shortly. The evening meal. Then sleep, if sleep would come. And in three days, the arena. The roar of fifty thousand voices. The smell of blood and fear and cheap wine. The emperor watching from his box, perhaps composing a poem about our deaths. I stood up, rudis in hand, and walked back into the sun. The wolf on the hilt seemed to snarl in the light. For now, it was enough. For now, I was still alive. Still breathing Rome's foul air. Still dreaming impossible dreams between the clack of wooden swords and the distant cheers of a crowd that would cheer just as loudly for my death. The eternal city turned and turned around us, grinding lives into legend, and we, its willing and unwilling sacrifices, kept feeding its endless hunger. (Word count: 1,012)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and atmospheric writing within the historical fiction genre. The author successfully creates an immersive Roman setting through carefully chosen sensory details - the "reek of the ludus" combining "sour straw, old sweat, cheap olive oil, and the iron tang of blood" immediately establishes both place and mood. The first-person narration maintains consistency throughout, presenting a weary, contemplative gladiator whose voice feels authentic to both his circumstances and the historical period. The characterization of the unnamed Thracian protagonist is well-developed, showing depth through his internal contradictions - a man who has adapted to survive while retaining fragments of his former identity. His relationship with his rudis serves as an effective symbolic anchor, representing both his captivity and his remaining agency. The supporting characters, while briefly sketched, feel authentic to the setting. The piece successfully integrates historical and political context without heavy-handedness. References to Nero's Golden House, the Great Fire of Rome, Christian persecution, and the broader "bread and circuses" dynamic feel naturally woven into the narrative rather than forced exposition. The author demonstrates solid research knowledge while avoiding the trap of overwhelming the reader with historical detail. The prose quality is generally strong, with effective use of concrete imagery and sensory details. Phrases like "Twenty-three years old and already I moved like an old man" and "The eternal city turned and turned around us, grinding lives into legend" show skilled writing. However, some passages veer toward overwrought territory, particularly in the final paragraphs where metaphors become somewhat heavy-handed. The emotional complexity is well-handled, presenting a protagonist caught between resignation and defiance, memory and survival. The piece avoids sentimentality while maintaining emotional resonance. The exploration of themes like dehumanization, survival, and the loss of identity feels earned rather than imposed. Structurally, the piece follows a clear chronological progression through a single day, maintaining focus while allowing for natural reflection and character development. The pacing works well for the contemplative tone, though some sections could be tightened. Minor weaknesses include occasional lapses into telling rather than showing, particularly in exposition about Roman politics, and some dialogue that feels slightly modern in construction. The ending, while thematically appropriate, relies somewhat heavily on metaphorical language that borders on purple prose. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 86.2
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Telegram – 12 March 1927** MOTHER STOP ARRIVED SKERRYVORE STOP LIGHTHOUSE STANDS LIKE A GREY FINGER IN THE SEA STOP SUPPLIES ALL PRESENT STOP WIND NEVER STOPS STOP YOUR SON **Letter – 3 April 1927** Dear Mother, The post boat finally came. Three weeks I waited. I’m sorry I only sent the telegram. The keeper before me left a half-bottle of whisky and a tin of condensed milk, both frozen solid. I thawed the milk on the stove and it tasted of rust. The tower is sound enough. Four hundred and twenty steps to the light. My legs know every one of them now. The wind here has a voice, Mother. Not like the Glasgow wind that just howls. This one talks low and personal, right against the glass. Sometimes it sounds like Da when he was in drink. The wireless set crackles more than it speaks. I get the shipping forecast and sometimes a bit of music if the weather’s kind. Last night they played “Annie Laurie” and I had to switch it off. Made me soft. I’ve put your photograph on the shelf above the stove. The one from Rothesay, 1919. You’re wearing that terrible hat with the cherries. I like seeing it. Makes the place less like a stone coffin. Your son, Callum **Letter – 21 May 1927** Mother, Three gales in a row. The lantern glass cracked on the south side. I had to lash myself to the railing to put a temporary patch on. Felt like the wind wanted to peel me off the tower like a limpet. I’ve started talking to the seals. There’s a big bull that comes most evenings. I call him Murdoch. He looks at me like he knows things. I told him about the time you hid Da’s whisky in the coal scuttle. He seemed impressed. The loneliness isn’t what I expected. It’s not dramatic. It’s just there, every hour, like a second heartbeat that’s slightly out of time with the first. I found something in the stores cupboard. A small wooden box, no bigger than a cigarette case. Inside is a perfect little ship made of bone and human hair. The sails are made from what looks like a woman’s plait. There’s a note in tiny writing: “Made by J. McAllister, 1893, during 47 days of isolation.” The hair is probably his wife’s. I keep taking it out and looking at it. Can’t decide if it’s beautiful or the saddest thing I’ve ever seen. I’m keeping it on the table. Sometimes I talk to it instead of Murdoch. Your son, Callum **Telegram – 9 July 1927** MOTHER STOP PLEASE SEND WOOL FOR SOCKS STOP MY OLD ONES HAVE HOLES BIG ENOUGH FOR RATS STOP ALSO ANY OF YOUR SHORTBREAD IF YOU CAN SPARE STOP CAL **Letter – 14 August 1927** Mother, The shortbread arrived in pieces but I ate every crumb like a savage. Tasted exactly like home. I sat on the gallery with the tin on my knee and cried like a wean. The gulls laughed at me. I’ve been thinking about Da a lot. The way he used to come home from the yards with that tight look on his face. I used to think he was angry at us. Now I wonder if he was just tired of being small in a big world. This place makes you feel very small indeed. The relief keeper is due in October but they’re saying the weather might delay him. I try not to think about it. I’ve named the bone ship “The Widow.” Don’t know why. Just feels right. The hair in the rigging catches the lamplight sometimes and looks almost alive. I keep catching myself stroking the sails when I’m thinking. Write soon. Tell me about the new neighbours. Tell me anything. Even what the cat had for its breakfast. Your son, Callum **Letter – 2 November 1927** Mother, They’re not coming. The relief boat can’t make the crossing. The seas are too high. The principal keeper radioed yesterday. Said I’m to “hold the station” until further notice. I laughed when I read the message. Laughed until I was sick over the railing. I keep the lamp turning. That’s the main thing. The light must turn. Everything else is just noise. I talk to The Widow now like she’s real. Told her about the girl I almost married in 1924. Margaret. You never liked her. You were right. She wanted a man who came home every night smelling of solid ground. Can’t blame her. Sometimes I think the wind is trying to tell me something important but I can’t quite catch the words. Other times I think I’m going mad and that’s fine too. I’m not sleeping much. The light turns, the foghorn moans, and I sit with The Widow on the table between us like we’re keeping watch together. I love you, Mother. I should have said it more when I was there. Callum **Letter – 19 December 1927** Mother, I’ve done something stupid. Three nights ago the wind dropped for the first time in months. The sea went flat as glass. The stars were so bright they hurt to look at. I took The Widow and climbed down to the rocks at low tide. I wanted to show her the real sea. I slipped. The box went into the water. I went in after it. The cold was like nothing I’ve ever felt. I got hold of the box but the current had me. I thought that was it. Then Murdoch appeared. The big seal. I swear he pushed me towards the rocks. I got my hands on the iron rung and dragged myself out. Lay there coughing seawater for an hour. The Widow is ruined. The bone has gone soft. The hair has come loose and floats in the box like drowned weed. I keep it anyway. Can’t throw it out. I’m so tired, Mother. Your son, Callum **Telegram – 3 January 1928** MOTHER STOP I AM WELL STOP DO NOT WORRY STOP THE RELIEF BOAT IS COMING NEXT WEEK STOP I AM READY TO COME HOME STOP CAL **Final Letter – 12 January 1928** Dear Mother, I’m writing this in the bothy on the mainland. The relief keeper arrived yesterday. Young lad called Robertson. Looked at me like I was a ghost. Maybe I am. They had to help me down the steps. My legs had forgotten how to walk on anything that didn’t sway. The doctor says I’m malnourished and “showing signs of nervous exhaustion.” Fancy words for going a bit mad in a stone tower. I still have The Widow. What’s left of her. I keep the box in my coat pocket. The hair has dried into strange patterns, like writing I can’t read. Sometimes I think if I stare at it long enough I’ll understand what it’s trying to tell me. I’m coming home on the Thursday train. I’ll be smaller than when I left. Quieter. But I’m coming. I’ve been thinking about what I’ll do next. The yards are no good for me anymore. Maybe I’ll get a job in the market, or maybe I’ll just sit in the kitchen with you and drink tea until the quiet feels normal again. Thank you for the letters. I know I didn’t always answer properly. Your words kept me from going completely under. That and The Widow. And Murdoch, wherever he is now. I’ll see you soon. Keep the kettle warm. Your son, Callum P.S. I’m bringing the wooden box. I thought maybe we could bury what’s left of her in the garden, under the rowan tree. She kept me company. Only seems right she gets to rest somewhere with proper earth and no wind.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative successfully captures the voice of a working-class Scottish lighthouse keeper through authentic dialect and vernacular without resorting to heavy-handed stereotyping. The progression from initial optimism through deepening isolation to psychological breakdown and eventual recovery feels organic and well-paced across the nine-month timeline. The characterization of Callum is particularly strong - his voice remains consistent throughout while showing believable psychological deterioration. His relationship with objects like "The Widow" (the bone ship) and Murdoch the seal demonstrates how isolation manifests in anthropomorphizing and seeking connection with non-human entities. The mother's presence is felt despite her voice being absent, which is skillful given the one-sided correspondence format. The imagery is vivid and atmospheric without becoming overwrought. Descriptions like "the lighthouse stands like a grey finger in the sea" and "the wind here has a voice" are evocative but grounded. The bone ship made from hair serves as an effective central metaphor for isolation, connection, and the previous keeper's own struggle. The story builds genuine emotional tension through Callum's deteriorating mental state, culminating in his near-drowning while trying to save the symbolic ship. This crisis point feels earned rather than manufactured. The resolution, while hopeful, acknowledges lasting damage - he'll be "smaller than when I left" - which avoids false optimism. The prose flows naturally with appropriate rhythm for letters and telegrams. The telegrams feel authentic to the period and medium. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the symbolism becomes slightly heavy-handed, and the seal rescue scene borders on convenient, though it's established early enough to feel acceptable. The story demonstrates creativity in its use of the bone ship as both literal object and metaphor, and in how it shows rather than tells the progression of isolation. The ending provides satisfying closure while maintaining emotional honesty about trauma and recovery. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 71.6
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Hacker Mindset** *Okay, breathe. This is not a pentest. This is a date. A normal, civilian, first-date experience. Stop casing the room like it’s a black-box engagement.* I’m sitting across from Alex in this little Italian place that smells like garlic and regret. Candle on the table, terrible acoustic playlist, the usual. But Alex is… unexpectedly not terrible. We’d spent the first twenty minutes in that awkward small-talk death spiral until I mentioned that I still have my Revised dual lands in a binder at home. Their eyes lit up like I’d just cast a Tinker for a Blightsteel Colossus. Forty minutes later we’re deep in a debate about whether the Legacy format is healthier than Modern and I haven’t once thought about the fact that the restaurant’s WiFi password—**“Marinara2023!”**—is literally taped to the host stand in 72-point Comic Sans for every script kiddie in a three-block radius to see. *Focus. They’re laughing. You’re laughing. This is going well. Do not ruin this.* I like the way Alex talks with their hands when they get excited about the new Duskmourn set. There’s this little scar on their left thumb that I’m trying very hard not to catalog as “possible defensive wound from a knife fight or maybe just a kitchen accident.” See? I’m growing. Self-improvement. They reach for their phone to show me a janky homebrew decklist they’ve been working on. That’s when it happens. Their unlock pattern. Jesus Christ. They don’t even angle the screen. Four quick swipes on a 3x3 grid: down the left side, across the bottom, diagonal back up. It’s basically the letter “Z” if Z had performance issues. I watch it happen in slow motion, the way I used to watch newbie sysadmins type passwords in cleartext over shoulder-surfable Zoom calls. *Four-node pattern. No complex shape. No random starting point. Predictable muscle memory. Phone’s at least two generations old so no biometric fallback enforced on every unlock. They’re using it one-handed while holding a wine glass. Jesus.* I feel the old reflex kick in like muscle memory of its own. My brain immediately starts building the attack tree: - Visual acquisition: already complete. - Thermal imaging on the glass would be overkill but technically possible. - Smudge attack viable if they ever clean the screen (they haven’t). - Social engineering vector: just ask them to unlock it for any reason and I’d have it. I hate that I know all this. I *really* hate that I can’t stop knowing it. They set the phone down and catch me staring at their hands. Their smile falters. “What?” they ask. I should lie. I should say nothing. Instead my mouth opens and the words fall out like unpatched buffer overflow. “Your unlock pattern is… really bad.” *You absolute fucking moron.* Alex blinks. “I’m sorry?” I can feel the temperature at the table drop ten degrees. The little voice that used to keep me alive in sketchy IRC channels is screaming *abort abort social situation compromised* but the reformed-white-hat part of me is trying to salvage. “I mean—look, I’m sorry, that was a weird thing to say. It’s just… I used to do security work. Penetration testing. The kind where you break things for money. And your pattern is… it’s basically the first thing I’d try if I were trying to get into your phone. Which I’m not. Obviously. That would be psychotic.” *Smooth. Real smooth. Nothing says “I’m emotionally available” like unsolicited vulnerability assessment of someone’s personal device.* Alex pulls their phone closer, suddenly self-conscious. “It’s… fine. I change it every few months.” *No you don’t.* “You don’t,” I say, and immediately want to reach into my own mouth and pull the words back out. “I mean—statistically people don’t. And yours has that little hesitation on the last node. You’re thinking about it. That means it’s not muscle memory for a new pattern. Which means it’s old. Which means—” I stop. Close my eyes. Try to remember what normal humans sound like. “I’m ruining this, aren’t I?” Alex is quiet for a long moment. They’re studying me the way I study network diagrams—looking for the weak points, the unexpected connections. “You can’t turn it off, can you?” they ask. Not angry. Curious. “The… seeing.” I laugh, but it comes out bitter. “I wish. I walk into restaurants and notice the WiFi password is on the counter like a goddamn buffet. I meet someone new and within five minutes I’ve unconsciously mapped their tells, their password habits, their likely recovery questions. I once broke up with someone because I noticed they used their childhood pet’s name as their Amazon password and then got mad at *me* for pointing it out. Which, in retrospect, fair.” I take a sip of water to give my mouth something to do that isn’t talking. “I really like you, Alex. Like, scarily like you for a first date. The Magic thing isn’t even the main reason, though it’s a really good one. You’re funny in that dry way that hits right in the sarcasm center of my brain. And I’m sitting here trying to have a human moment and all I can think about is how I could probably unlock your phone in under thirty seconds with just visual observation and a little social engineering.” I gesture vaguely at the host stand. “Also the WiFi password is Marinara2023 with an exclamation point and I’m having a small stroke about it.” Alex looks over, sees the sign, and actually laughs. A real one. The tension breaks like a poorly implemented access control list. “That *is* a terrible password,” they admit. “It’s not even a password, it’s a cry for help.” They’re quiet again, but this time they’re smiling a little. “So what would you suggest?” I groan. “Don’t. Do not ask the weird hacker on a first date to do a security consultation. That’s how I end up in this exact situation every single time.” “But you’re clearly in pain. It’s like watching someone with OCD not straighten a crooked painting.” They’re not wrong. My leg is bouncing under the table. “Fine. At minimum, make it a non-linear pattern. Start from the center, go to an edge, double back through a used node if your phone lets you. Better yet, just use a proper password or passphrase and enable biometrics properly. And for the love of whatever god watches over nerds, clean your screen sometimes so I can’t literally see your old patterns in the oil.” Alex nods solemnly, like I’m giving them the nuclear codes. Then they pick up their phone, tilts it away from me theatrically, and very obviously changes their pattern while making exaggerated “secret” faces. “There,” they say, setting it down. “New pattern. Completely different. You have no idea what it is.” “I could probably guess it in three tries,” I say, then immediately add, “but I won’t. Because that would be terrifying and also I’m trying to be a better person who doesn’t treat every human interaction like a red team exercise.” They lean forward, elbows on the table. “You know what’s funny?” “That I’m single at thirty-four?” “No. Well, yes, but no.” Their eyes are sparkling. “I work in incident response. Blue team. I spend all day trying to explain to developers why their ‘password123’ is bad and why storing secrets in client-side JavaScript is a war crime. I thought I was going to have to spend the whole date pretending not to notice tech things.” The relief that floods through me is ridiculous. “So when I said your pattern was bad…” “You were right,” they admit, grinning. “It was terrible. I’ve been meaning to change it for months. But you still could have waited until at least the second date to do a full security review of my personal devices, you massive dork.” “Second date?” I ask, trying not to sound too hopeful. “Depends,” they say, picking up the dessert menu. “You going to tell me what other terrible habits you’ve noticed tonight, or are you going to try to have a normal conversation about whether the new Murders at Karlov Manor set was a mistake?” I consider this. Look at the WiFi password again. Look at Alex, who’s watching me with something that looks suspiciously like affection instead of the usual “please stop talking” expression I’m used to. “I make no promises,” I say finally. “But I’m willing to attempt human connection if you’re willing to accept occasional unsolicited threat modeling as a love language.” They smile. It’s a good smile. “Deal. Now help me decide between tiramisu and the chocolate cake. And if you say anything about how the point-of-sale system is probably running Windows XP, I’m leaving.” I don’t tell them the point-of-sale system is running Windows XP. But I do notice. (Word count: 1002)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative understanding of the prompt. The author successfully captures the hacker mindset through authentic technical details and realistic internal monologue that feels genuinely informed by cybersecurity knowledge. The protagonist's inability to "turn off" their analytical perspective is convincingly portrayed through specific observations about WiFi passwords, unlock patterns, and security vulnerabilities. The narrative structure works well, building tension through the protagonist's internal struggle between wanting human connection and being compelled to notice security flaws. The Magic: The Gathering shared interest provides believable common ground, and the technical jargon feels natural rather than forced. The voice remains consistent throughout - neurotic, self-aware, and technically minded but emotionally vulnerable. However, the piece suffers from some significant weaknesses. The resolution feels somewhat too neat and convenient. The revelation that Alex works in incident response provides an easy solution to what should be a more complex interpersonal challenge. This coincidence undermines the story's exploration of how the protagonist's compulsions affect relationships. The ending positivity, while charming, doesn't feel fully earned given the severity of the protagonist's social dysfunction described earlier. The dialogue, while generally competent, occasionally feels stilted, particularly in Alex's responses. Some exchanges read more like exposition delivery than natural conversation. The piece also tends toward over-explanation at times, with the protagonist's internal monologue becoming repetitive about their condition rather than deepening our understanding of it. The technical details are well-researched and authentic, but the emotional complexity could be deeper. The protagonist's self-awareness about their issues is perhaps too complete, making them more likeable but less psychologically realistic. Real behavioral compulsions are typically more resistant to rational self-analysis. The prose is generally clean and readable, with some effective moments of humor and self-deprecation. The pacing works well, building to the phone pattern revelation and managing the aftermath competently. The piece succeeds in being entertaining and engaging, even if it doesn't fully explore the darker implications of its premise. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 14 Nuanced Characters: 13 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 11 Unearned Transformations: 10 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 15
