+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 82.8
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**From: Angus MacAllister, Sule Skerry Lighthouse** **To: Mrs. Margaret MacAllister, 14b Miller Street, Glasgow** **15 March 1927** Dear Mother, The relief boat came yesterday so I’m sending this with the lads before they head back. I’m settled now. The cottage is smaller than I remembered from the interview, but it’s dry enough. The light itself is a brute of a thing, four tons of brass and glass that needs polishing every day or the salt eats it. I’ve got the rhythm of it already: up at four, trim the wicks, climb the tower twice more before dark. The sea is loud. That’s the main thing I notice. It never stops talking. I’m keeping fine. Tell Da the tobacco arrived safe. I’ll write again when the next boat comes. Your son, Angus --- **From: Margaret MacAllister, Glasgow** **To: Mr. Angus MacAllister, Sule Skerry Lighthouse** **2 April 1927** My dear Angus, I was glad to get your note. Your father says the tobacco was a kind thought. The house is quiet without you. Mrs. McKinnon from the next close asked after you again. I told her you were keeping the light for sailors and she looked at me like I’d said you were keeping bees on the moon. Write when you can. I worry about the damp. You always had a chest. Your loving mother, Maggie --- **From: Angus** **To: Mother** **27 April 1927** Mother, The wind is something else out here. Yesterday it came from the north-east so hard the rain went sideways. I found a dead gull jammed in the lantern room window this morning, poor beast. I buried it below the rocks like a Christian. The loneliness is not what I expected. It’s not that there’s nobody to talk to. It’s that the nobody is so complete it starts to feel like a person itself. I’ve started talking to the light. Not daft talk, just explaining what I’m doing. “Right then, hen, let’s get you polished.” It passes the time. The paraffin is low again. I don’t know how the last keeper managed. I’m measuring every drop. Angus --- **From: Margaret** **To: Angus** **12 May 1927** Angus, You never said the paraffin was short. I spoke to Mr. Campbell at the Northern Lighthouse Board when I was in town. He says Sule Skerry is always short because the boat can’t always land. You’re to be careful. I don’t like the thought of you sitting in the dark. Your father’s cough is back. The doctor says it’s the same old dust from the yards. He asks if you’re eating properly. I told him lighthouse keepers don’t get fat. Write soon. The house is too quiet. Mother --- **From: Angus** **To: Mother** **3 June 1927** Mother, I had a proper fright last week. The fog came down thick as porridge for four days. I kept the foghorn going but the compressor started knocking. I had to strip it down with a lantern between my knees while the horn screamed over my head every thirty seconds. My hands were shaking so bad I dropped a valve into the works. Spent six hours fishing it out with a length of wire and a lot of swearing. When the fog finally lifted I saw the steamer *Loch Carron* had passed within half a mile. She must have heard the horn but she was that close I could see the men on her deck. If the horn had failed... I don’t like thinking about it. The solan geese are nesting on the western rocks. Noisy bastards. I’ve named the loudest one after Uncle Tam because it never shuts up. Angus --- **From: Margaret** **To: Angus** **20 June 1927** My darling boy, I didn’t sleep after your last letter. Your father says I’m being daft but I know that tone. You’re not right. I’ve made up a parcel with carbolic soap and some of my fruitcake. The Board says it’ll go out with the next relief. You eat every crumb. Your cousin Willie’s gone to Canada. He says there’s work for men who aren’t afraid of the cold. I told him my son was already colder than any Canadian and twice as stubborn. Be careful with that compressor. I’ve prayed for you every night since you left. I know you think that’s daft too. Mother --- **From: Angus** **To: Mother** **14 July 1927** Mother, I’ve been trying to write this for a week. I don’t know how to say it without sounding soft. There’s a seal that comes to the landing steps most evenings. Big old bull with a scar across his nose. I started leaving him the skins from the potatoes. Now he waits for me. I talk to him the way I used to talk to you when I was wee and had done something stupid. He looks at me with those wet eyes and I swear he understands more than most people ever did. I’m not sleeping much. The light has to be perfect. Every night I think about that steamer in the fog and what would have happened if I’d failed. The responsibility is crushing the breath out of me. Some days I stand at the top of the tower and the sea looks like it wants me. Not in a daft way. Just... it’s patient. It can wait. I’m not going to do anything stupid. I promised you that when Da had his accident. But Christ, Mother, it’s hard. Angus --- **From: Margaret** **To: Angus** **29 July 1927** Angus MacAllister, You listen to me now. You are not the first man to keep that light and you won’t be the last. Your grandfather kept the light at Rattray Head for twenty-three years and he used to cry like a bairn when the boat came to relieve him because he didn’t want to leave. The sea does that to people. It gets inside them. I’ve spoken to Mr. Campbell again. He says the next relief is in September and they’ll send a new man if you need it. But he also says the best keepers are the ones who feel it. The ones who don’t feel it are usually the ones who let the light go out. You keep that light burning, son. And you keep that seal fed. I’m sending more potatoes. Your mother --- **From: Angus** **To: Mother** **18 August 1927** Mother, The compressor seized completely last week. I had to hand-crank the foghorn for nine hours straight when the fog came down again. My right arm is still trembling. I thought my heart was going to burst. When the fog finally cleared I sat on the steps and cried like a wean. The seal came right up beside me. I put my hand on his head and he let me. I don’t know what that means but it felt important. I’ve decided something. I’m going to ask for a wireless set. I know they’re expensive but if I can hear voices sometimes, even just the shipping forecasts, it might keep me from losing the place altogether. I’ll pay it back out of my wages. I’m not giving up. Not yet. Angus --- **From: Margaret** **To: Angus** **4 September 1927** Angus, Mr. Campbell says the wireless can be arranged. It’ll be on the next boat along with a new compressor. He also says you’re one of the better keepers they’ve had in years. I didn’t tell him you cry with seals. Some things a mother keeps to herself. Your father’s been bad this week. The doctor thinks it might be his heart. I haven’t told him about your troubles. He’s proud of you. Talks about his son the lighthouse keeper to anyone who’ll listen. The parcel has warm socks and a tin of your favourite toffee. Eat it slowly. Mother --- **From: Angus** **To: Mother** **22 October 1927** Mother, The wireless is a miracle. Last night I heard a concert from Glasgow. A woman singing “My Love is Like a Red Red Rose” and I swear I could smell the close and hear the neighbours shouting. I sat there with the seal snoring on the rocks below and I didn’t feel alone for the first time in months. Your last letter worried me. Tell Da I’m coming home for Christmas if they’ll let me. I’ve not asked yet but I will. I need to see you both. The light is steady. I’m steady with it now. Some days are still bad. The wind still talks too much. But I’m learning the difference between loneliness and being alone. There’s a difference. The seal has a name now. I call him Hamish. He seems to like it. Your son, Angus --- **From: Margaret** **To: Angus** **11 November 1927** Angus, Your father passed on the 3rd. It was quick at the end. He spoke about you. Said to tell you the light was yours to keep now, whatever that means. I’m not asking you to come home. I know what that light means to you now. But I wanted you to know he was proud. I’m proud. The wireless was a good idea. I’m glad you have the music. I’ll be fine. The women from the kirk have been round. You know what they’re like. Write when you can. The house is quiet but I’m not lonely. Not really. I talk to your father sometimes. I think he hears me. Your mother --- **From: Angus** **To: Mother** **Christmas Day 1927** Mother, The boat came yesterday with your parcel and a letter from the Board saying I can have Christmas leave next year if I want it. I sat on the steps with Hamish and read your letter three times. I lit the lamp at sunset like always. But this time I spoke to Da while I did it. Told him the light was steady and that I finally understood what he meant about carrying something bigger than yourself. The sea is calm tonight. First time in weeks. The stars are so clear you could read by them. I played the wireless after dinner and heard a choir singing “In the Bleak Midwinter.” I cried again but it was different this time. I’m keeping the light, Mother. For Da. For you. For the sailors I’ll never meet. And for myself, I suppose. I’ll see you in the spring. Until then I’m sending all my love in this letter and asking you to keep some for me. I’ll need it when I come home. Your son, Angus MacAllister Keeper of the Sule Skerry Light (Word count: 1002)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative successfully fulfills most of the prompt requirements, creating a compelling story of isolation and gradual emotional development through correspondence between a Scottish lighthouse keeper and his mother. The piece demonstrates strong adherence to the specified format, spanning several months with distinct voices for both characters. The Scottish working-class voice is authentically rendered without resorting to heavy dialect or stereotypes. Angus's voice evolves naturally from initial stoicism to vulnerability and eventual acceptance, while his mother Margaret maintains a consistent maternal tone that balances concern with practical wisdom. The progression feels organic rather than forced. The story's emotional arc is well-constructed, moving from initial adjustment through deepening isolation, crisis, and ultimately to a hard-won peace. The introduction of the seal Hamish and the wireless radio serve as effective plot devices that don't feel contrived. The father's death provides genuine emotional weight and serves as a catalyst for Angus's final transformation. The imagery is particularly strong - the "four tons of brass and glass," the fog "thick as porridge," and the sea that "never stops talking" create vivid sensory experiences. The writing avoids purple prose while maintaining poetic moments that feel earned. The detail about the dead gull and the compressor repairs adds authenticity to the lighthouse setting. However, the piece has some minor weaknesses. The resolution, while emotionally satisfying, edges toward being slightly too neat. The Christmas Day letter, though moving, risks sentimentality. Some transitions between emotional states could be more gradual, particularly Angus's shift from despair to acceptance. The characterization is nuanced, particularly in showing how isolation affects different aspects of Angus's personality. His relationship with the seal and his conversations with the lighthouse equipment reveal psychological depth without explicit exposition. Margaret's character, while less developed due to the format constraints, shows wisdom and strength that feel genuine rather than idealized. The prose flows naturally with varied sentence structures that suit each character's voice. The piece maintains engagement throughout, building tension effectively toward the emotional climax of the father's death and Angus's ultimate acceptance of his role. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 5 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 78.7
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**I shouldn't be doing this.** The thought loops in my head like a buffer overflow I can't patch. I'm sitting across from Elena at this little Italian place on 5th, the kind with the Edison bulbs and exposed brick that tries too hard to feel authentic. She's laughing at something I said about the old Urza block, and for thirty glorious seconds I actually forget to map the blind spots in the waitstaff's movements or calculate how many seconds it would take me to own the point-of-sale system if I really wanted to. *Focus, asshole. Human connection. That's the quest today.* She's gorgeous in that way that makes my pattern-matching brain short-circuit. Dark curls, sharp eyes, the kind of smile that suggests she's three moves ahead in every conversation. And she plays Magic. *Actually* plays it. Not the "I watched one Pro Tour once" cosplay. Real, competitive, knows-what-a-fetchland-is Magic. This date is going stupidly well. *Her left hand is on the table. No wedding ring tan line. Good. Phone is face-down next to her water glass. iPhone 15 Pro. The screen has those micro-scratches that tell me she doesn't use a screen protector. Interesting. The case is a little loose on the bottom right corner—* No. Stop. I force my attention back to her face. She's talking about her favorite Limited format and how she misses the days when you could actually draft a coherent archetype without getting completely fucked by bombs. I nod, genuinely interested for once. My brain, that treacherous pattern-recognition engine that's been my curse since I was fourteen, is busy doing two things at once: listening to her and cataloging. *She just mentioned playing Grixis midrange in Pioneer. Values card advantage and interaction over pure aggression. Prefers to grind out games. Control player at heart. Interesting.* The waiter brings our appetizers. I watch him enter the order on his tablet. The way he holds it, the angle—Jesus Christ, I could shoulder-surf that PIN in two seconds. I hate myself for knowing that. Elena is telling me about her most memorable tournament moment, some insane topdeck against a Yawgmoth player that involved a timely *Orcish Bowmasters* and a *Sheoldred*. I laugh in all the right places because it's actually funny, and because she's animated when she talks about it, hands moving like she's physically manipulating the stack. *Her laugh has a slight delay before the real one, like she's checking to see if you're safe first. Trust issues? Or just careful? The way she holds her fork—dominant hand, but she switches when she's thinking. Ambidextrous tendencies. Adaptable.* I take a sip of water and try to remember what normal people do on dates. They don't mentally diagram the restaurant's network topology or notice that the security camera over the bar has a dead zone covering three tables including ours. They certainly don't— Her phone lights up. It's face down but the screen is bright enough that when it illuminates, I can see the unlock pattern she just drew to check the notification. Nine dots. She went 1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8-9 in a perfect Z formation with a little hook at the end that suggests she sometimes adds a tenth point for security. *For fuck's sake.* The pattern is terrible. I could crack that in under five attempts from muscle memory after seeing it once. The smudges on her screen would probably give it away to anyone who knows what they're looking at. My brain immediately starts building the attack: shoulder surfing, smudge attack, thermal imaging if I had the right equipment, just watching her do it three more times. I could own that phone in thirty seconds. I hate that I know this. I hate that I can't *not* know this. She catches me staring at her phone. "Everything okay?" she asks, tilting her head. That sharp look again. She's perceptive. Of course she is. *Abort. Abort. Social engineering recovery protocol.* I could lie. I could say I thought I saw a spider. I could play it cool. Instead, because my brain-to-mouth filter has the security of a Windows 95 machine running ActiveX, I hear myself say: "Your unlock pattern is... noticeable." *God fucking damn it.* The temperature at the table drops about ten degrees. Her expression doesn't change much, but I see the micro-adjustments. Pupils contracting. Shoulders shifting back half an inch. The "oh, this motherfucker" micro-expression that only someone who's spent years reading people would catch. She looks at her phone, then back at me. "Excuse me?" *Damage assessment: Critical. But maybe recoverable. She's a nerd. Play it as a nerd thing.* I rub the back of my neck, feeling the scar from that one time in Barcelona when things went very wrong. Old habits. "Look, that came out wrong. I'm sorry. It's just... I used to do... security work. The kind where you notice things you really wish you didn't. Your pattern is Z-pattern with the hook. It's common. The smudges on your screen would give it away to anyone paying attention. I'm not saying that to be creepy, I'm saying it because I physically cannot stop seeing it and it's making my brain itch." She stares at me for a long moment. I can see her running her own analysis. *Threat assessment. Is this guy dangerous or just socially maladjusted?* *She mentioned playing a lot of Dimir in Standard last year. Blue-black. Deception, control, information advantage. She's evaluating whether to counterspell this conversation or let it resolve.* Finally she says, "You realize that's an incredibly weird thing to say on a first date, right?" "Trust me, I'm aware. My internal monologue has been screaming at me for the last forty-five seconds." She leans back, studying me. "What kind of 'security work' exactly?" *Truth or strategic omission? She's smart enough to detect bullshit.* "Used to break into things. Systems. Networks. Sometimes buildings. Then I decided I'd rather be the person who stops that from happening. The transition has been... incomplete. My brain didn't get the memo." I can feel myself cataloging more cards from our earlier conversation while my mouth is trying to salvage this disaster. *She mentioned liking to play The Locust God in Commander. Token swarm with draw. Values engine building. When she talked about her favorite deck being an older version of Lantern Control, that was telling—prison strategies, disrupting opponent's plans rather than racing. She likes to be the one in control of the game's tempo.* Elena is quiet for a moment, then surprises me by laughing. Not the polite laugh. The real one. "You're actually broken, aren't you?" "Terminally," I admit. "I noticed the waiter has his tablet PIN set to 1234. I noticed that the couple at table seven is having some kind of financial argument based on their body language and the fact that she just checked her banking app three times. I noticed that your Magic collection probably has a heavy emphasis on foils because you mentioned 'the way the light hits the art' twice. And yes, I noticed your terrible phone security." She picks up her phone, looks at the screen, then at me. "It's not *that* bad." "It's objectively bad. I could crack it in three tries. Four if I'm being polite." *She's smiling. This might not be completely fucked.* "Prove it," she says, sliding the phone across the table. "Without looking at the smudges or anything. Just from what you saw." *Challenge accepted. But this feels like a test.* I don't touch the phone. "9-5-1-3-7-9 with the little flourish on the 9 that suggests you sometimes add the 6 for extra security but didn't this time. The pattern is too predictable because you think in symmetrical shapes." Her eyebrows go up. "Jesus." "I know. It's exhausting. You have no idea how much I wish I could turn it off and just... enjoy the fact that a beautiful, terrifyingly competent woman wants to talk about Magic with me." She considers this. Takes a sip of her wine. I notice she's left-handed when she drinks. The other data point slots into my mental model. *She mentioned playing a lot of control mirrors. She's comfortable in long, drawn-out information wars. This date just became one.* "Okay," she says finally. "New rule. For the rest of this date, every time you notice something you shouldn't, you have to tell me what Magic card it reminds you of." I blink. "That's... actually a really good containment strategy." "Like what would my phone pattern be?" "Probably something like *Pithing Needle*," I say without thinking. "It looks innocuous but fucks very specific things." She grins. It's a dangerous grin. "And what deck would you play against someone like me?" *She's flirting with the monster. Interesting.* "Probably some janky *Norin the Wary* chaos deck. Something that does the opposite of what you expect. Make you play the game wrong." Elena laughs again, and this time it's warm. She slides her phone back into her bag—actually puts it away properly, screen down, in the inner pocket. A small act of trust that makes something in my chest do complicated things. "Alright, reformed hacker. Tell me about your worst *Oops, All Spells* moment while I decide if I'm going to let you buy me tiramisu or if this date dies in a fiery spectacular crash." *She mentioned playing Jeskai Ascendancy combo in Modern before. She likes when the pieces come together in unexpected ways. Maybe this date is the same.* I start talking, and for the first time in years, my brain's constant vulnerability scanning feels almost... useful. Like maybe I can turn the curse into something that looks like connection. *She's going to destroy me in Limited if we ever play. I can't wait.* The internal monologue keeps cataloging—*she likes blue for its flexibility, black for its efficiency, has a competitive streak but values creativity over netdecking*—but now it feels less like a security audit and more like learning a new deck. Maybe that's what this is. A new format. One where the win condition is something other than root access. *God, I hope I don't fuck this up.* But I'm already building the decklist in my head. Sideboard and everything.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative understanding of the prompt. The author successfully captures the mindset of a reformed hacker through convincing internal monologue that reveals both technical expertise and social awkwardness. The Magic: The Gathering shared interest feels authentic and serves as an effective bridge between the characters. The characterization is particularly strong. The protagonist's compulsive pattern recognition and vulnerability assessment feels genuine, with specific technical details that demonstrate research and understanding. Elena emerges as more than a plot device - her responses feel realistic and her interest in the protagonist's quirks rather than immediate rejection adds complexity to the interaction. The prose maintains consistent voice throughout, balancing technical jargon with accessible narrative. The internal monologue format works well, allowing insight into the protagonist's fractured attention between genuine human connection and involuntary security analysis. The Magic metaphors are cleverly integrated and feel natural rather than forced. However, the piece has some structural weaknesses. The resolution feels somewhat rushed and overly optimistic given the severity of the social transgression. While Elena's acceptance of the protagonist's behavior is not entirely unbelievable, the speed of her transition from concern to flirtation could be more gradual. The ending, while charming, borders on wish fulfillment. The dialogue, when present, feels authentic and serves character development well. The technical details are convincing without becoming overwhelming, though some readers might find the hacker terminology alienating. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly the protagonist's self-awareness about their condition and genuine desire to connect despite their compulsions. The piece succeeds in creating tension and maintaining reader interest throughout. The restaurant setting is efficiently established without excessive description, and the pacing generally works well despite the internal monologue format potentially slowing narrative momentum. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 15.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 6.0, Weak Dialogue: 4.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 7.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 4.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8.0, Unearned Transformations: 7.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Overthinking It** Okay. Breathe. Just breathe. In through the nose, out through the mouth like Dr. Patel said in that stupid mindfulness video we watched in Health last month. I’m sitting at the lab table and my knee is already bouncing under it like it has its own nervous system. Mrs. Alvarez just announced the groups for the ecosystem project and of course—because the universe has a cruel sense of humor—I got paired with him. Elias. The boy whose last name I’ve practiced writing in the margins of my notebook so many times I could probably forge his signature if I ever needed to. Which I never would. That would be insane. I’m not insane. I’m just… me. He’s sliding into the seat next to mine right now and my stomach does that roller-coaster drop thing. I keep my eyes glued to my notebook like it’s the most fascinating thing in the entire lab. I’ve already started doodling. Tiny sketches, almost microscopic so no one can tell what they are if they glance over. There’s a little cartoon version of Priya in the corner—glasses, ponytail, holding a beaker like a trophy because she always acts like she already knows the answer. Next to her is Marcus, drawn as a stick figure with giant headphones because he never takes them off even when Mrs. Alvarez yells. And then there’s Elias. I’ve given him fluffy hair that actually looks like his hair, which was a mistake because now it feels too accurate and therefore suspicious. He’s just a few lines and dots but somehow he’s smiling in the drawing the same way he smiles in real life, like he knows a secret and it’s probably funny. I press the mechanical pencil so hard the lead snaps. Great. Perfect. Now I look like I’m aggressively drawing nothing. *Say something normal. Normal people say normal things when they’re paired with their crush for a science project. Ask about his weekend. No, that sounds like I’m fishing for information. Ask if he’s seen the rubric. Too obvious. Everyone’s seen the rubric. Compliment his hoodie? No. Desperate. What if he thinks I’m staring at his clothes? What if he thinks I notice what he wears every day because I do notice what he wears every day and that makes me a creep?* My throat is closing up. I can feel the words I rehearsed in the shower this morning crowding behind my teeth like they’re all trying to get out at once and none of them want to go first. *Hi, I’m really glad we’re partners. No. Too eager. Hey, partner. God, that’s worse. Sounds like I’m in a corporate team-building exercise.* Elias taps his pencil on the table twice, like a drumbeat. “So… biome or aquatic ecosystem? I vote biome. More animals.” His voice is right there, aimed at me, and my brain short-circuits like an overloaded circuit board. I’ve read six different articles on temperate rainforests in preparation for this exact moment and now I can’t remember the difference between a biome and a biosphere. Excellent. Top-tier intelligence at work. *Say something smart. You’re smart. You’re the girl who corrected Mrs. Alvarez about the nitrogen cycle in October and everyone still remembers it. Use that. Be useful. Be chill. Be the version of you that exists inside your head instead of this trembling mess wearing your skin.* “Biome,” I manage. It comes out like a croak. One syllable. I sound like a frog who’s been studying for the wrong test. I clear my throat and try again, staring at my doodle of him like it might give me courage. “I mean, yeah. Biome. There’s more… variables? With the food chains and stuff.” *Variables? Did I just say variables? We’re not even at the data part yet. I sound like I’m trying to impress him with basic vocabulary. Next I’ll tell him I know what a hypothesis is. Maybe I should just slide under the table and live there forever. The floor looks clean enough. I could photosynthesize. Become a plant. Plants don’t have crushes. Plants don’t rehearse conversations for three days straight and then say the wrong one word.* He’s nodding though. Not laughing. Not looking at me like I’m a alien who learned English from watching TED Talks. “Cool. We could do the taiga. Lots of cool adaptations. Like how some trees have needles instead of leaves.” He’s talking to me. Actually talking. And I’m nodding so hard I probably look like one of those bobblehead dolls. My face feels hot. Is it hot in here? Is the lab on fire? Should I check the emergency shower station? No. Focus. He said taiga. I know everything about the taiga. I read an entire National Geographic article last night at 11:47 p.m. while telling myself it was for school and not because I wanted to have something to talk to him about. *Tell him about the permafrost. No, that’s boring. Tell him about the lynx and snowshoe hare population cycles. But what if he already knows that? Then I look like I’m showing off. What if he doesn’t know and thinks I’m a know-it-all? What if my voice cracks on the word “population” and he never wants to be my partner again and I have to spend the rest of the year as That Girl Who Got Weird About Lynxes?* I realize I haven’t said anything for too long. The silence is stretching like a rubber band about to snap back and hit me in the face. I grab my pencil and start shading the tiny Elias doodle’s jacket so I have something to do with my hands. My real hands are shaking. The ones in the drawing are perfectly steady. Lucky fictional me. “I like… the snow,” I say. *The snow?* “I mean, the way the taiga handles snow load on the branches. The conical shape. It’s… efficient.” *Efficient. I just called a forest efficient. I should be institutionalized.* Elias leans a little closer to look at my notebook and I freeze like a deer in headlights. He’s going to see the doodles. He’s going to see miniature him with the stupid fluffy hair and he’s going to know. He’s going to know that I drew him with more care than I drew anyone else and that I gave him a tiny smile and that I’ve been sitting here for four minutes trying not to combust. But he just points at the corner of the page. “Is that supposed to be Marcus with the headphones? That’s funny. You’re really good at the little drawings.” My heart is doing something complicated. Part of me is glowing—he noticed, he thinks they’re good, he said “you’re really good”—and the other part is screaming *abort mission he’s looking at your notebook he’s two inches from your arm he smells like laundry detergent and something woodsy and I’m going to spontaneously combust right here in third period.* “It’s nothing,” I mumble. “Just… doodling. Helps me think.” *Helps me think? It helps me not think about how I want to know what your handwriting looks like up close and whether you’d think my jokes were funny if I could ever actually tell you one without sounding like a malfunctioning robot.* Priya is already organizing our shared Google Doc from across the table, assigning roles in her bossy-but-nice voice. I’m supposed to be the researcher. Of course I am. The quiet girl gets to disappear into books and articles where she doesn’t have to make eye contact. Perfect. Safe. Except Elias is the visual aid person and that means we have to talk about what goes on the poster and I’ll have to sit next to him for at least three more classes and every time our elbows almost touch I’ll have a small internal meltdown. I start a new doodle in the bottom margin. This one is of me—tiny, round glasses, hair in the braid I actually wore today, clutching a stack of note cards that say “DON’T BE WEIRD” in microscopic letters. I draw a speech bubble coming out of her mouth but leave it empty because I can’t even imagine what the right thing to say would be. Elias is writing our names at the top of the planning sheet. He writes my name first. *Sophie.* He spells it right. Most people put a y. He didn’t. He knows how I spell my name. That tiny detail feels enormous, like he’s been paying attention too. Or maybe he just looked at the class list. Probably the class list. I’m being delusional. I need to stop reading romance novels before bed. *What if I just said something real? What if I said “I get nervous talking to people but I’m really glad it’s you and not someone who’ll make me do all the work”? No. That’s honest and honesty is social suicide. What if I asked him what music he’s listening to lately? But then he might play me something and I’ll have to have an opinion and what if my opinion is wrong? What if he likes rap and I only know indie folk songs about forests which is ironically perfect for this project but also deeply uncool?* My pencil is drawing tiny pine trees around the border now. Each one is a little different. Some are bent under imaginary snow. Some are protecting tiny animals. I draw a miniature version of Elias standing next to one, looking up at the branches like he’s thinking about adaptations. The real Elias is humming under his breath. I don’t recognize the song. I want to ask. I don’t ask. Instead I say, “I can find the articles on keystone species if you want. For the poster.” It’s eight whole words. A personal record. My voice only shook on the last one. He smiles. The same smile I gave his doodle. “That’d be awesome, Sophie. We’re gonna crush this.” *He said my name. He said we. Like we’re a team. Like he’s not secretly counting down the minutes until he can go sit with his actual friends and tell them how the quiet girl talked about efficient snow shapes.* I duck my head and go back to doodling before he can see that my face is approximately the color of the emergency fire blanket hanging on the wall. Inside my head the conversation continues, articulate and funny and brave. Inside my head I tell him that I’ve had a crush on him since he explained the water cycle with those dumb hand gestures in September. Inside my head I make him laugh and we discover we both secretly love terrible sci-fi movies and he thinks my drawings are cool and not at all creepy. In real life I shade another tiny pine tree and try not to vibrate out of my own skin. The bell is going to ring in thirty-seven minutes. I have thirty-seven minutes to exist next to him without imploding. I’ve already used four of them to say the word “efficient” like a total weirdo. I start another doodle. This one is of my own brain, drawn as a tangled ball of yarn with little Post-it notes stuck all over it that say things like “DON’T SAY ANYTHING STUPID” and “HE CAN HEAR YOUR HEARTBEAT” and “MAYBE JUST BECOME A HERMIT AFTER THIS.” I’m so tired. I’m so tired of monitoring every single word, every micro-expression, every way I sit or breathe or hold my pencil like it might reveal that I like him. That I like him so much it feels like there’s a whole other ecosystem living in my chest, complete with its own food chain of panic and daydreams and catastrophic thinking. Elias passes me the planning sheet. Our fingers don’t touch but they come within three inches of each other and that’s enough to short-circuit my thoughts again. *Say thank you. Say anything.* “Thanks,” I whisper. He grins. “No problem, partner.” *Partner. Partner. Partner.* I draw one more tiny sketch while he’s not looking. Just a tiny heart, almost invisible, hidden inside the branches of the smallest pine tree. No one will ever see it. Not even me after today, because I’ll probably rip this page out and eat it if he ever asks to look at my notes. The internal monologue keeps going, looping, rehearsing, rewriting every possible future conversation we might have between now and the due date. My outside self stays very still, drawing tiny worlds no one else can see, while my inside self screams in eight different directions at once. I’m exhausted and we’re only on day one. I can’t wait for tomorrow. I’m terrified of tomorrow. I shade the heart until it disappears completely into the paper, just another secret buried in the taiga of my notebook. (Word count: 1002)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong understanding of the assignment and delivers an authentic portrayal of teenage social anxiety through internal monologue. The writer successfully captures the exhausting mental gymnastics of an overthinking adolescent, creating a believable and relatable character whose intelligence contrasts sharply with her social paralysis. The narrative structure works well, anchored in the specific setting of a science class group project. The author effectively uses the doodles as both a coping mechanism and a metaphorical device, showing rather than telling the character's emotional state. The progression from panic to tentative hope feels natural and unforced. The voice is consistently authentic throughout - the mix of sophisticated vocabulary in internal thoughts contrasted with stilted external speech rings true for an intelligent but anxious teenager. Phrases like "I sound like a frog who's been studying for the wrong test" and "Maybe I should just become a hermit after this" capture the character's self-deprecating humor and catastrophic thinking patterns. The imagery is particularly strong in the doodle descriptions, which serve multiple narrative functions - revealing character, advancing plot, and providing concrete details that ground the abstract anxiety. The taiga metaphor works on multiple levels, connecting the academic content to the character's emotional landscape. However, the piece occasionally veers toward being slightly overwrought in its anxiety portrayal, with some repetitive thought patterns that, while realistic, could have been trimmed for better pacing. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - neither falsely optimistic nor unrealistically dark, but capturing the genuine ambivalence of the situation. The dialogue, though minimal, feels authentic to how teenagers actually speak in classroom settings. The character's internal commentary on her own speech patterns adds layers of self-awareness that enhance rather than detract from the realism. The piece succeeds in creating genuine emotional engagement through specificity and authenticity rather than melodrama. The writer demonstrates skill in maintaining tension throughout a relatively static scene, using internal conflict to drive the narrative forward. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 6 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
