+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 80.7
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #: SCP-████** **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** The office previously occupied by Dr. Elias Hart (now designated Site-19 Anomalous Observation Chamber 14) is to remain sealed under Level 3 temporal surveillance. All furniture, documents, and personal effects are to be left exactly as they were at the time of Dr. Hart’s disappearance. A standard-issue steel desk, one chair, and a single overhead fluorescent fixture are the only permitted objects within the chamber. Entry is restricted to personnel with O5 approval and must occur in pairs. All sessions are to be recorded by three redundant camera systems operating on separate power grids and data streams. Any visual anomaly, power fluctuation, or momentary dropout in even one camera feed is to be treated as a potential manifestation event. Notes discovered on the desk are to be photographed in situ, handled with nitrile gloves, scanned at 1200 dpi, then placed in a locked evidence locker inside the chamber. No note may be removed from the room for more than eleven minutes. Dr. Hart’s personnel file has been flagged with a memetic hazard warning. Any Foundation employee who begins experiencing intrusive thoughts regarding “future correspondence” or who reports finding handwritten notes in their workspace is to be immediately placed on psychological leave and transferred to Site-17 for amnestic screening and possible SCP-████ exposure testing. **Addendum ████-A: Incident Log (Dr. Hart’s Personal Account)** I never meant to start a log like this. I’m a researcher, not a diarist. But after the fourth note I stopped trusting my own memory, and after the seventh I stopped trusting my own eyes. This is my attempt to keep some record while I still can. It began on 12 March 20██. I had stayed late to finish the quarterly review on the new batch of Class-D assigned to SCP-173. The rest of the wing was empty. I remember the exact time because the clock on my monitor read 23:47 when I stood up to stretch. When I turned back, a single sheet of my own stationery was centered on the desk. It had not been there thirty seconds earlier. The handwriting was mine. I would know it anywhere; the cramped, slightly left-leaning script I developed after breaking my right index finger in grad school. The note was written in black ballpoint, the same pen I keep in my breast pocket. The first note read: *Elias – Do not open the observation window tomorrow at 09:15. Whatever you do, keep 173’s chamber sealed. They’re wrong about the eye contact protocol. It learns. —You, 14 March 03:22* I laughed. It was a nervous laugh. I checked the security footage immediately. **Camera footage exhibits a 0.8-second total dropout at 23:47:12.** All three independent systems glitched simultaneously. When the feeds returned, the note was on the desk and I was staring at it with a stupid expression. I told myself it was a prank. Maybe one of the junior researchers. Maybe I had written it myself during a micro-sleep and simply didn’t remember. I locked the note in my drawer and went home. The next morning I opened the observation window at 09:15 exactly as scheduled. Nothing happened. SCP-173 remained motionless. The test concluded without incident. I felt relieved and also strangely disappointed, the way one feels when a predicted disaster fails to materialize. That night I found the second note. It was waiting when I returned from the cafeteria. Same paper. Same pen. The handwriting was shakier. *You didn’t listen. Of course you didn’t. I wouldn’t have either. It already escaped in the timeline that matters. At 11:09 it will be in the ventilation. You still have time to seal sublevel 2. Burn this note. Don’t show it to anyone. They’re watching the cameras for the flicker. —You, 13 March 04:11* The date on the note was yesterday’s. I sat down heavily. My hands were shaking so badly I dropped the paper. When I picked it up again I noticed something that made my stomach fold in on itself: the paper had a small blood smear in the bottom right corner. My blood type. I checked my fingers. No cuts. **All three security feeds glitched at 23:51:44.** I called Dr. Patel, my immediate superior. I told her I thought I was having a psychotic break. She arrived within ten minutes, took the note, and immediately ordered a full psychological evaluation. While we waited for the on-site psychiatrist, she asked to see the security footage. The glitch was there. Clear as day. Three different codecs, three different timestamps, all blank for almost a full second. Dr. Patel’s face changed. She stopped treating me like a colleague having a breakdown and started treating me like an anomaly. They moved me to temporary housing on-site that night. I was placed under observation. I begged them to let me keep writing. They allowed it on the condition that every page went straight to the secure locker inside the original office. The notes kept coming. Each time the cameras died for precisely 0.7 to 1.1 seconds. Never the same length. Always at night. Always when I was alone or when the only other person present was asleep or looking away. The handwriting deteriorated rapidly. Third note (14 March): *Seal the damn vents Elias. It’s already in the walls. I can hear it clicking behind the plaster while I write this. You still think this is a joke. I’m running out of paper and time. Whatever you do, do not trust the version of me that tells you the breach was prevented. That version is lying or already dead.* Fourth note (15 March, handwriting almost illegible): *They think I’m crazy. They’re right but not the way they think. The thing in 173 isn’t a statue. It’s a door. Every time we blink it opens a little wider. I blinked for four years. You will too. The other researchers are already replaced. Patel’s eyes don’t close properly anymore. I saw.* I started keeping my own hidden journal, the one you’re reading now. I wrote it in the margins of mundane research documents so the Foundation wouldn’t confiscate it immediately. I began to notice that some of the notes contradicted each other and, worse, contradicted established reality. One note insisted that Dr. Patel had died in the breach on the 16th. Another note, dated the 17th, referenced a conversation I had with her on the 18th that I have no memory of. A third note claimed I had already been killed and that the person writing the notes was some future iteration trying to close the loop by warning me not to exist. The Foundation launched an investigation into whether this was an infohazardous memetic entity using me as a vector, perhaps something related to SCP-████’s own properties or even a previously undocumented aspect of SCP-173. They brought in a temporal anomalies specialist from Site-01. Dr. Lang. Dr. Lang’s hypothesis was that I had somehow become an anchor point for a collapsing causal loop. He believed the notes were genuine, but that the future they described was one in which the Foundation itself had already lost. The contradictions were not madness, he said, but the symptoms of a timeline trying to heal itself by tearing out infected sections. I asked him if that meant I was going to cause the breach by trying to prevent it. He didn’t answer. On the 19th the notes stopped being warnings and became pleas. *Please stop looking at the cameras. Every time you check the footage you make the glitch worse. You’re teaching it how to hide. I’m so tired Elias. I haven’t slept since the 11th. My hands won’t stop shaking. I keep finding myself in corridors I don’t remember walking down. Last night I found a note I hadn’t written yet. It was in my own pocket and it said “you’re already too late.” I burned it but the ashes spelled out your name.* The final note was different. It wasn’t on my desk. It was in my quarters, under my pillow, while I was being interviewed three floors away with two armed guards present. The cameras in my quarters showed the same 0.9-second dropout. The handwriting was not mine anymore. It was close, but the loops were wrong. The pressure on the page was that of someone on the verge of a seizure. *It isn’t me writing these anymore. It hasn’t been me for days. I thought I was saving you but I was only delivering the infection. Close the loop, Elias. Burn the office. Kill the version of us that keeps trying. There is no breach. There never was. We are the breach.* I stared at that note for a long time. Then I did something I’m not proud of. I lied to the Foundation. I told Dr. Lang and the security team that I believed it was a psychological attack by an intelligent SCP we had yet to identify. I suggested we attempt to use the notes to lure the entity into a trap. They agreed to let me return to the office one final time, under heavy guard. At 03:12 this morning I sat at the desk and waited. The cameras glitched. When the feed returned, a new note was on the desk. The handwriting was perfect. Calm. My handwriting on my best days. It read: *Good. You finally stopped fighting it. Now we can begin.* I looked up at the camera that had just rebooted. For half a second I thought I saw myself standing behind the glass, smiling at me. Then the image resolved and there was only the empty corridor. I haven’t written a note since. They found this document this afternoon, hidden in the lining of my mattress. I don’t remember writing most of it. The desk is still waiting. The cameras are still watching. I don’t know which me is writing this anymore. But I know one thing with absolute certainty: The next note is going to be the last. And I think I’m going to leave it for you. —Dr. Elias Hart *Final Entry, 21 March 20██* **Addendum ████-B:** Following the discovery of the above document, Dr. Hart was placed in solitary containment. At 04:44 the same morning, all three camera systems recorded a simultaneous 1.3-second blackout. When visual was restored, Dr. Hart was missing. The only object present on the desk was a single sheet of paper. The note is written in ink the color of dried blood. It says: *See you yesterday.*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of the SCP Foundation format while delivering a genuinely unsettling psychological horror narrative. The piece successfully blends the clinical, bureaucratic tone of SCP documentation with the increasingly desperate first-person account of Dr. Hart, creating an effective contrast that heightens the horror. The structure is well-crafted, beginning with standard containment procedures that immediately establish something has gone very wrong, then transitioning into Hart's personal log. This framing device works exceptionally well, as it allows the reader to experience both the Foundation's clinical perspective and Hart's deteriorating mental state. The concept of temporal notes is executed with considerable creativity. Rather than simply presenting a straightforward time loop, the author introduces contradictions and inconsistencies that blur the line between temporal anomaly and psychological breakdown. The notes themselves evolve convincingly from coherent warnings to desperate pleas to something more sinister, with the handwriting deterioration serving as an effective visual metaphor for Hart's declining state. The prose maintains an appropriate clinical tone throughout most of the piece, with Hart's voice remaining believable as that of a researcher trying to document an impossible situation. The technical details about camera systems, timestamps, and security protocols add authenticity to the Foundation setting. However, the piece suffers from some pacing issues in the middle section, where the repetitive nature of note discovery becomes somewhat predictable. The psychological deterioration, while well-executed, follows a fairly standard trajectory that experienced horror readers will anticipate. The ending is genuinely chilling, with the final note "See you yesterday" providing an excellent capstone that implies the temporal loop's completion. The ambiguity about whether Hart has become part of the anomaly or was consumed by it maintains the uncertainty that drives the piece's effectiveness. The technical aspects of the writing are generally strong, with natural sentence flow and appropriate vocabulary for the setting. The piece avoids purple prose while still creating atmospheric tension through precise, clinical language that becomes increasingly unstable as Hart's situation deteriorates. + Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 77.0
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #: SCP-████** **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** All instances of SCP-████ are to be secured under the guise of condemned or structurally unsound buildings. Access is restricted to Level 4 personnel with O5 approval. Each confirmed door is to be fitted with a standard Foundation containment lock and monitored by motion-activated infrared cameras. Under no circumstances are civilian explorers, urban explorers, or non-Foundation archaeological teams to be allowed near any instance. Following Incident ████-Alpha, any personnel found to have entered SCP-████ without authorization, including members of the O5 Council, are to be administered Class-B amnestics and placed on administrative leave pending psychological review. **Description:** SCP-████ designates a series of identical, unmarked wooden doors that manifest without warning inside abandoned or condemned structures across the globe. The doors are indistinguishable from one another: plain oak, no hardware, no signage, and approximately 2.1 meters in height by 0.9 meters in width. When the surrounding area is in complete darkness, a faint, steady white glow is visible escaping through the edges of the door. Each door opens into an architectural structure designated SCP-████-1, an apparently infinite archive of shelves, corridors, and reading rooms that defies known Euclidean geometry. Internal mapping attempts using drones, laser rangefinders, and cognitohazard-resistant survey teams have consistently failed to produce consistent floor plans. The archive contains an estimated minimum of 400 million individual documents, books, blueprints, photographs, and data storage devices. The vast majority of materials within SCP-████-1 reference a global human civilization that existed prior to recorded history. This civilization appears to have possessed advanced paratechnology and maintained a containment organization functionally identical to the Foundation. Recovered documents repeatedly refer to this organization as “The Oversight Committee” or “Oversight.” The cause of its collapse is uniformly described only as “the Containment Failure of ████.” The following excerpts were recovered during Exploration Log ████-Beta: > **Recovered Document 17-Theta** > “By order of the Oversight Council, the designation of SCP-████ is hereby retired. The anomaly has been reclassified as Explained. All records of the previous civilization are to be sealed. We will not repeat their mistakes.” > *Handwritten note in margin (modern English, dated three weeks prior to recovery): They already have.* > **Recovered Personnel File #3921** > “Dr. Evelyn Mercer, Senior Researcher, Site-19. Assigned to SCP-████ since [REDACTED]. Excellent field performance. Demonstrated moral flexibility during the 19██ containment of SCP-████. Recommended for promotion to Site Director. > *Note appended in different ink:* Still has the same nervous laugh. Still doesn’t remember me.” Similar files have been found for 41 current Foundation employees and researchers, all dated between 300 and 800 years ago. In every case the physical description, psychological profile, and even handwriting match the modern individual exactly. **Exploration Log ████-Beta (excerpts):** Exploration Team “Archivist-1” (Dr. L. Harlan, Agent M. Kowalski, Agent P. Ruiz, Dr. S. Abara) entered SCP-████-1 via an instance located in an abandoned textile mill in Łódź, Poland. The door was discovered after reports of “ghost light” in the building. Team was equipped with standard exploration gear, GPS (non-functional inside), and low-light recording equipment. > **00:00:00** – Door opens into a long hallway lined with metal shelves. The air is dry and smells of ozone and old paper. The faint glow from the doorway behind them is the only light source until headlamps are switched on. > **Dr. Harlan:** “It’s cold. Like a meat locker. The glow is still visible even with the door open. That shouldn’t happen.” > **00:07:19** – Team reaches a circular chamber approximately 40 meters in diameter. Thousands of filing cabinets radiate outward like spokes. Agent Ruiz locates a cabinet containing personnel files. The first file pulled bears Dr. Harlan’s face and current Foundation ID photo. The file is dated 1843. > **Dr. Harlan:** (quietly) “This is my signature. I don’t remember writing this. I don’t remember ever being in Poland before today.” > **00:21:44** – Distant metallic sounds heard. Team proceeds toward the noise. The architecture becomes increasingly impossible; corridors loop back on themselves at 45-degree angles that should not exist. > **00:28:12** – Visual contact with entities. Three figures in matte-white hazmat suits with opaque visors are observed removing entire shelves using what appear to be portable spatial compressors. The entities work in complete silence. When the exploration team rounds the corner, all three figures freeze. One makes a quick hand signal. All three then drop their equipment and flee down a side corridor that was not present three seconds earlier. Pursuit is attempted but the corridor vanishes behind the team, replaced by a solid wall of shelves. > **Recovered Note found at scene:** > “They’re watching us watch them. The archive is being cleaned. We are the contamination. Do not trust the O5s who claim they have never been here. At least three of them have their own files. They know what the Failure was.” > *Signed with an O5 designation that has been redacted by an unknown party.* > **00:41:55** – Team discovers a sealed wing labeled “Oversight Archive – Level 0 Clearance Only.” The door is unlocked. Inside are twelve chairs arranged around a circular table. Each chair has a small brass plate. The names are current O5 designations. The chair labeled O5-3 contains a half-finished cup of coffee that is still warm. > **Dr. Abara:** “This was brewed less than an hour ago. Someone is using this place like a private reading room.” > **00:49:12** – Further exploration is aborted when the team begins encountering their own footprints leading in the opposite direction, despite never having walked that path. The footprints are fresh and contain traces of blood. **Addendum ████-Gamma:** On ██/██/20██, O5-█ was found unconscious in their private quarters with trace amounts of SCP-████-1 archival dust on their shoes and hands. When questioned, O5-█ claimed no memory of leaving their office. A search of their quarters uncovered a single sheet of paper containing only the words: > “We were the Oversight Council. > We failed. > You are not the first Foundation. > You will not be the last.” The paper was written in O5-█’s own handwriting. Research into the precise nature of “the Containment Failure of ████” is ongoing. All exploration of SCP-████-1 has been suspended indefinitely. Despite the suspension order, security footage at three separate SCP-████ instances has recorded unidentified individuals in O5-issued black suits entering the doors at 03:00 local time. The doors remain. The glow continues to leak into the dark. (Word count: 1002)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while weaving a compelling narrative of temporal loops and organizational horror. The piece successfully captures the bureaucratic tone characteristic of SCP entries, maintaining consistency throughout the formal containment procedures, exploration logs, and recovered documents. The concept is genuinely creative - doors leading to an archive of a previous civilization's Foundation equivalent creates an unsettling mirror that reflects the current organization's potential future. The inclusion of personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago, and the revelation that O5 Council members are secretly visiting, builds effective paranoia and questions about identity and cyclical history. The exploration log format works well, providing concrete details through the team's discoveries while maintaining the clinical distance expected in Foundation documentation. The progression from mundane discovery to increasingly impossible architecture to the revelation of active removal by hazmat entities creates good pacing and escalating tension. However, the piece suffers from some structural issues. The characters in the exploration team are largely interchangeable voices delivering exposition rather than distinct personalities. Dr. Harlan's reaction to finding her own file is appropriately unsettling but brief. The dialogue serves primarily functional purposes rather than revealing character depth. The writing occasionally becomes repetitive in its mysterious implications - multiple instances of "they know more than they're saying" without sufficient variation in how this is presented. Some of the recovered documents feel slightly forced in their ominous implications, particularly the margin notes that seem too conveniently placed. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity for an SCP entry while providing a satisfying sense of ongoing dread. The final image of the continuing glow is effective. The piece successfully avoids over-explaining its central mystery while providing enough concrete details to make the threat feel real and immediate. The technical execution is solid with good attention to SCP formatting conventions, though some of the redactions feel arbitrary rather than purposeful. The word count adherence is precise, and the piece maintains its unsettling atmosphere throughout without becoming overwrought. + Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 83.0
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 3: The Color of Estrangement** The shuttle’s landing lights had barely cooled before the official itinerary clamped down like a harness. Kade Tessera, junior envoy of the Ekumen’s Diplomatic Service, endured the greetings, the antiseptic speeches, the ritual exchange of credentials beneath the pearl-gray sky of Vire. Everything was as the briefings had promised: orderly, tense, and slightly too polite. The colony’s three registered subspecies—Terran-norm, Longstride, and Drift—were represented in careful rotation at the reception. Their delegates smiled with mouths that no longer quite matched one another. Kade lasted four hours. Then, citing a need for “atmospheric acclimation,” ve slipped the consular attaché, left the slate-gray diplomatic quarter, and walked alone into the city. Vire’s capital was called Low Harbor, though the sea had retreated three kilometers in the last two centuries. The streets sloped gently toward a memory of water. Kade moved with the unhurried pace of someone trying not to look like an offworlder, though the attempt was hopeless. At one hundred and seventy-three centimeters ve was short even among the Terran-norm population, and vis skin still carried the even olive tone of a person raised under the filtered light of Hain. Here, sunlight struck harder and carried a metallic edge. The market district began without warning. One moment Kade was walking between poured-stone buildings; the next, canvas awnings flared overhead like the wings of roosting birds. The air smelled of yeast, hot iron, and something sharp and mineral that ve could not name. Stalls spilled across the cobbles. A woman with the elongated limbs and narrow chest of the Longstride lineage sat cross-legged on a mat, weaving at a back-strap loom no wider than her own torso. Her fingers moved with the economical grace of someone who had done this since she could sit upright. Beside her, bolts of cloth were stacked like loaves of bread. The dyes were what stopped Kade mid-step. On Earth such colors did not exist outside laboratories. Here they were commonplace. A deep violet that seemed to breathe, shifting between indigo and bruised plum as the angle of light changed. A green that carried the cold fire of glacial melt. A red so saturated it looked wet. Kade reached out, then caught verself and withdrew the hand. “Bloodroot and tide-copper,” the weaver said without looking up. Her voice was low, almost conversational. “The red comes from a lichen that only grows on the eastern scarps. The green is copper salts fixed with kelp ash. You won’t have seen them.” “I haven’t,” Kade admitted. The woman lifted her gaze then. Her eyes were the pale gold common among Longstrides, adapted for the long twilight of Vire’s winters. She studied Kade the way one studies an unfamiliar bird. “You’re the one from the stars.” “I am.” “You smell of recycled air.” Kade laughed despite verself. “I probably do.” The weaver returned to her shuttle. “Walk softly, star-child. The streets here remember more than the ministers do.” Kade moved on, deeper into the market. Ve bought a small loaf of black bread from a Drift vendor whose skin carried the faint opalescent sheen of those whose ancestors had lived for generations in the high valleys where UV was merciless. The man’s eyes were protected by a second, transparent lid that flicked once as he counted out change. He did not smile, but neither did he stare. That alone felt like courtesy. Everywhere Kade looked, the official categories frayed. A child with Longstride height but Drift eyes clung to the hand of a stocky Terran-norm woman who might have been her mother, aunt, or genetic co-parent; the relationship was clearly intimate and clearly none of Kade’s business. Two old men—one Drift, one Longstride—played a board game on an upturned crate, trading insults in a dialect that mixed all three lineages into something new. A street singer with the barrel chest and resonant voice of the high-plains Drift performed an old Earth ballad in a minor key that made the hair on Kade’s arms rise. None of this had been in the briefings. The briefings spoke of “manageable divergence” and “productive tension.” They did not mention that the Longstrides had begun to refer to themselves, half in jest, as *the people who walk far*, or that certain Drift families had quietly revived matrilineal naming practices that had been extinct on Earth for nine hundred years. They certainly did not mention that the market’s dyers were forming guilds that crossed subspecies lines and were beginning to speak, softly, of representation on the colonial council. Kade felt the weight of what ve was seeing settle behind vis sternum like a second heart. When ve returned to the diplomatic residence the sky had gone the color of tarnished pewter. The attaché was waiting in the foyer, face rigid with disapproval. “Envoy Tessera. You were expected at the mineralogy exhibit.” “I sent my regrets.” “Command wishes to see you.” Kade had expected the reprimand. Ve had not expected it to come from Minister Soren Vale himself. The Minister of Science occupied a suite on the top floor that had once been an observatory. The telescopes were gone now; the dome had been converted into an office whose curved walls displayed shifting holographic star charts. Vale stood at the center of the room like a man who had chosen his backdrop with care. He was pure Terran-norm, silver-haired, impeccably tailored, and possessed of the sort of calm that comes from having survived three previous administrations. “Envoy,” he said, without turning. “You smell of the market. Bloodroot and black bread, if I’m not mistaken.” Kade felt a flicker of respect. “Your nose is better than the weaver’s.” Vale faced ver at last. His eyes were the color of wet slate. “I have spent forty years learning to notice what the official reports omit. You appear to have done it in four hours. That is either very promising or very dangerous.” “Perhaps both.” A small smile. “Sit.” Kade remained standing. “I’d rather not, if it’s all the same. I’ve been sitting through briefings for weeks.” Vale studied ver for a long moment. Then he did something unexpected: he shrugged out of his formal jacket, hung it over the back of a chair, and rolled his sleeves to the elbow with the economy of a man who had once done manual labor. “You’re young,” he said. “Twenty-eight standard? Twenty-nine?” “Twenty-seven.” “Even worse.” He gestured to the star charts. “Do you know what the Ekumen fears most about Vire?” “Divergence.” “Wrong. The Ekumen fears *uncontrolled* divergence. There is a difference. Controlled divergence produces interesting art and useful genetic data. Uncontrolled divergence produces war. Or worse—secessionist ideologies wrapped in the language of cultural destiny.” Kade felt the conversation tilt beneath ver like a deck in high wind. “The people in the market didn’t seem interested in war,” ve said carefully. “They seemed interested in cloth. And in not being told who they are allowed to marry.” Vale’s expression did not change, but something behind his eyes sharpened. “You spoke with the weaver.” “I did.” “She is the cousin of a woman who sits on the Longstride advisory council. Did you know that?” “No.” “Of course not. You were too busy being moved by the romance of the authentic.” Vale’s voice was gentle, almost regretful. “That is the trap, Envoy. The market is not innocent. Nothing here is innocent. Every color those dyers use is also a political statement. The lichen that makes their sacred violet only grows on land the Drift claim as ancestral. The copper comes from mines the Longstrides have worked for two centuries. Every purchase is a tiny treaty. Every refusal is a tiny declaration of war.” Kade felt vis pulse in vis throat. “Then why does the official briefing insist that relations are stable?” “Because the official briefing is written by people who need stability more than they need truth.” Vale stepped closer. He was taller than Kade had realized. “I am not a scientist, whatever my title says. I am a political operator who happens to understand that science is the only language all three subspecies still pretend to respect. My job is to keep the pretense useful. Your job, should you choose to accept it, is to decide whether you will help me maintain that usefulness or whether you will become another romantic who tears the web because the threads look ugly up close.” The room was very quiet. The star charts turned above them in silence. Kade drew a slow breath. The smell of the market still clung to vis clothes—bloodroot, copper, yeast, humanity in all its stubborn variation. “I won’t lie in my reports,” ve said. “I’m not asking you to lie. I’m asking you to *understand* before you write them.” Vale’s voice dropped, almost kind. “You slipped your leash today because you wanted to see what we’re afraid of. Very well. You’ve seen a piece of it. Now the question is whether you have the stomach to see the rest without deciding too quickly who the villains are.” Kade met the older man’s gaze. Something hot and stubborn rose in vis chest. “I don’t believe in villains, Minister. I believe in people trying to make sense of what they’ve become.” Vale studied ver for a long time. Then, unexpectedly, he smiled. It was not a pleasant smile, but it was real. “Good,” he said. “Then tomorrow you will accompany me to the high valleys. The Drift council has requested an audience, and they have specifically asked that the offworlder be present. Try not to smell too much like their market when you arrive. It makes them sentimental.” He turned back to the star charts. “You’re dismissed, Envoy. And Tessera—” Kade paused at the door. “Next time you decide to disappear, at least have the courtesy to take a tracker. I’d hate to lose you before I’ve decided whether you’re an asset or a liability.” Kade left without answering. In the hallway the lights seemed harsher, the air thinner. Behind ver, in the darkened observatory, Minister Soren Vale stood motionless beneath a hologram of unfamiliar constellations, a man calculating the exact tensile strength of a society that was learning, slowly and painfully, to become something the Ekumen had never planned for. Outside, night had fallen. Somewhere in the market district a singer was still performing, voice rising like smoke against the metallic stars. The song was old, but the accent was new. Kade stood listening until the last note faded, then turned toward vis quarters with the taste of black bread still on vis tongue and the certain knowledge that the briefings had been worse than useless. They had been lies of omission. And ve was, suddenly, ravenously hungry for the truth. (Word count: 1002)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Le Guin's style and thematic concerns, successfully capturing her measured prose, anthropological perspective, and exploration of cultural complexity. The author effectively employs Le Guin's characteristic techniques: the use of invented pronouns (ve/vis), careful world-building through observed details, and the presentation of political complexity without heavy-handed moralizing. The narrative structure is well-executed, moving from the sterile diplomatic environment through Kade's unauthorized exploration to the confrontation with Minister Vale. The market sequence is particularly strong, showing rather than telling the reader about the colony's social dynamics through concrete details like the weaver's colors, the mixed-heritage child, and the cross-subspecies board game. Character development is nuanced, especially in the portrayal of Minister Vale, who emerges as neither villain nor hero but as a pragmatic operator navigating genuine complexity. Kade's characterization as an idealistic but not naive young diplomat feels authentic, and their responses to both the market experience and Vale's challenge ring true. The dialogue is sharp and purposeful, particularly Vale's testing of Kade. Lines like "You smell of recycled air" and "Walk softly, star-child. The streets here remember more than the ministers do" feel natural while advancing both character and theme. The conversation between Kade and Vale crackles with tension and reveals character through subtext. The prose maintains Le Guin's characteristic restraint while still being evocative. Descriptions like "sunlight struck harder and carried a metallic edge" and "canvas awnings flared overhead like the wings of roosting birds" create vivid imagery without becoming overwrought. The writing demonstrates control and precision. The piece successfully avoids several common pitfalls: there are no unearned transformations, the ending maintains appropriate complexity rather than false positivity, and the political situation is presented with genuine nuance. The author resists the temptation to provide easy answers or clear moral divisions. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Certain phrases occasionally veer toward the precious ("vis pulse in vis throat"), and some world-building elements feel slightly mechanical in their presentation. The pacing in the middle section could be tighter, and while the market exploration is well-executed, it follows a somewhat predictable pattern of "naive outsider discovers hidden complexity." The thematic resonance is strong, exploring questions of cultural evolution, political control, and the tension between stability and authenticity that are central to Le Guin's work. The piece engages seriously with ideas about divergent human development and the politics of difference without reducing them to simple allegory. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 16.0, Emotionally Engaging: 15.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 7: Polymerase Chain Reaction** The basement beneath the old agri-dome smelled of ozone, damp concrete, and the faint sweetness of bioluminescent rot. Glow-in-the-dark mushrooms—*Agaricus lux* v3.2, the last thing HelixCorp ever released before the Collapse—pushed fat, pale caps through cracks in the floor tiles. Their cold blue-green light was the only illumination Jax allowed himself after midnight. Power was too precious to waste on LEDs. He hunched over the jury-rigged thermal cycler, a Frankenstein machine he’d rebuilt from three dead thermocyclers, a salvaged car battery, and the heating element ripped out of a corporate field-printer. Every forty-five seconds the thing screamed like a dying gull as the Peltier modules fought to cycle between 95 °C and 55 °C. The PID controller was a hacked child’s toy microcontroller; it drifted five degrees every run. Good enough for government work, as the saying used to go. There was no government now. The contract had arrived two days earlier, sewn inside the lining of a courier’s jacket: a black-market chit for twelve grams of viable, heritable wheat. Not the sterile F1 hybrids that Helix and its competitors had flooded the world with before the Crash. Real wheat. The kind that could be saved, replanted, and still produce seed the following year. The kind that broke three separate patents and one very dead corporation’s business model. The buyer was the River Collective, two hundred kilometers down the irradiated valley. They had water. Jax’s settlement, the Hollow, had nothing but dust and mouths. Twelve grams of seed for four hundred liters of potable water and two working solar sheets. The math was simple. Starve slowly or risk everything on one PCR run. Jax rubbed his left forearm where the sleeve had ridden up. The skin there was mottled violet and silver, a side-effect of the last “medicine” he’d cooked for himself—an untested plasmid cocktail meant to upregulate DNA repair after radiation exposure. The treatment had worked; his tumors stopped growing. The unexpected upside was that he could now taste magnetic fields. The downside was that his left hand sometimes forgot how to stop shaking. Trade-offs were the only currency left. He pipetted the last of the degraded template DNA into the reaction mix. The material had come from a vacuum-sealed pouch stamped with an expiration date twenty-three years past. Most of the strands had fragmented into useless junk, but a few intact cassettes remained—*Taq* polymerase scavenged from a dead man’s insulin pump, dNTPs leached from old COVID test kits, and primers he’d designed by eye on the back of a ration wrapper. The polymerase chain reaction was an act of faith now. Multiply the good bits. Pray the bad bits don’t multiply faster. The cycler beeped its ugly beep. Thirty cycles. He watched the mushrooms pulse in time with the machine, a coincidence that still felt like communion. While the reaction ran, Jax limped to the far bench where his “pharmacy” grew in repurposed salad crisper drawers. Six months ago he’d spliced a human growth-hormone analog into *Lactobacillus* cultured from his own gut. The bacteria lived in a nutrient slurry of boiled bark and rat droppings. Every morning he drank a shot-glass full. The stuff had regrown the cartilage in his ruined knee. It had also grown strange fibrous nodules along his spine that ached when it rained. Another upside, another tax. He swallowed today’s dose. The taste was indescribable. His vision sharpened almost immediately, colors separating into vivid, almost painful bands. The blue-green of the mushrooms flared like runway lights. Back at the cycler, the final extension step finished with a mechanical sigh. Jax cracked the lid. The tube was warm, alive with possibility. He ran a quick gel—agarose melted over a camping stove, stained with the juice of glow-mushrooms because real ethidium bromide was a fairy tale. The bands were there. Messy, but there. The wheat genes had amplified. He let himself smile for three full seconds before the fear rushed back in. Cloning the amplicons into a stable vector was the dangerous part. The only plasmid he had left was a stripped-down pUC19 derivative that had been through too many generations itself. He ligated in the dark, squinting by mushroom light, fingers trembling from the repair cocktail. The transformation mix went into electrocompetent *E. coli* he kept in the fridge next to the fermented beans. The electroporator was a piezo igniter from a plasma lighter hooked to a capacitor bank. When he triggered it, the spark was bright enough to leave afterimages shaped like corporate logos. The transformed bacteria went onto agar plates poured in Petri dishes that had once held breast implants. He slid the plates into an incubator made from a polystyrene cooler and a heating pad. Twenty-four hours. If he saw colonies, he would have seed. If not, the River Collective would get their chit back in a body bag. Jax sat on the cracked tile floor among the glowing fungi and waited. His knee throbbed in harmony with the cycler’s cooling fans. He scratched at the silver patches on his arm and wondered, not for the first time, whether he was any different from the corporations he hated. He too was engineering life with built-in obsolescence—his own body a patchwork of half-tested fixes that would probably kill him before forty. But the Hollow would eat next winter. Children who had never tasted bread that didn’t come from a corporate printer would taste it and know it could be grown again. That was worth a few extra tumors. From the incubator came the faintest smell of bacterial metabolism, like warm yeast and wet pennies. Hope smelled like contamination. He closed his eyes. In the blue-green dark the mushrooms kept growing, patient and indifferent, heirs to a world that had tried to patent life and failed. Jax rested his shaking hand on the warm metal flank of the cycler and felt the faint magnetic tug of the earth’s field singing through his altered nerves. Somewhere above, the wind moved through the skeletal remains of the agri-dome, whistling across broken glass and rusted corporate slogans. Down here, in the basement, something ancient and rebellious was being born again, one fragile base pair at a time. The PCR machine clicked off with a final, exhausted sigh. Jax smiled in the mushroom light, violet skin glowing like the fungi around him, and began the next run. (Word count: 1002)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk chapter demonstrates strong technical knowledge and worldbuilding, creating a convincing post-apocalyptic laboratory setting with detailed scientific procedures. The author clearly understands PCR methodology and genetic engineering concepts, integrating them naturally into the narrative rather than using them as mere window dressing. The protagonist Jax is well-developed through his actions and physical modifications, showing rather than telling his expertise and desperation. The body horror elements—violet and silver skin, magnetic field sensitivity, spinal nodules—effectively illustrate the theme of self-experimentation and biological modification. His motivations are clear and compelling: survival of his settlement through dangerous genetic work. The prose maintains a consistent dystopian tone throughout, with evocative imagery like the bioluminescent mushrooms providing atmospheric lighting and the jury-rigged equipment painted in convincing detail. Technical descriptions feel authentic without becoming overly expository. The writing successfully balances scientific accuracy with narrative momentum. However, the piece suffers from some overwrought passages, particularly in the final paragraphs where the prose becomes somewhat purple ("something ancient and rebellious was being born again, one fragile base pair at a time"). The ending, while thematically appropriate, edges toward unearned optimism given the precarious nature of the situation—Jax's smile and hope feel slightly incongruent with the established desperation. The chapter lacks dialogue entirely, which limits character development opportunities but isn't necessarily detrimental given the solitary laboratory setting. The structure meanders somewhat, jumping between the PCR procedure, worldbuilding details, and Jax's medical modifications without always maintaining tight narrative focus. The worldbuilding concept of planned obsolescence in genetic engineering is creative and well-executed, providing strong thematic foundation. The physical toll and unexpected benefits of genetic modification are convincingly portrayed through Jax's condition.
